content: The book 'Anatomy of Movement' was originally published in French as 'Anatomie pour le mouvement' by Editions Desiris in 1985. The English language edition of 'Anatomy of Movement' was published in 1993 and 2007 by Eastland Press. The book was translated by Regine MacKenzie. The book was edited by John O'Connor and Allan Kaplan. The cover illustration was done by Sandy Johnson. The cover design was done by Patricia O'Connor. The book design was done by Gary Niemeier.
summary: Details about the publication history, translation, and design of the book 'Anatomy of Movement'.

content: This book is an introductory text on basic human anatomy as it relates to external body movement. The book focuses on bones, muscles, and joints. The book does not describe the skull, visceral organs, circulatory system, or central nervous system. The book is designed to be compact and avoid repetition. Parts of the body affected by the same muscles may be described together. Reference may be given to a previous page where a structure or function is described in more detail.
summary: Description of the book's scope, content, and organization.

content: Drawings usually show structures from the right side of the body, with exceptions clearly indicated. Joints are sometimes drawn without adjacent bones to show articular surfaces. Each muscle is drawn in isolation to make its function more obvious.
summary: Explanation of the visual style and conventions used in the book's illustrations.

content: Chapter 1 provides basic orientation and terminology and should be read first. Subsequent chapters are arranged in a logical order (trunk, arm, leg) and should be read in order. The index is helpful for locating the page where a structure is first mentioned or described in detail.
summary: Guidance on how to read and navigate the book.

content: The anatomy of movement in the human body mainly involves the interaction of bones, joints, and muscles.
summary: The fundamental components involved in human movement.

content: Description of movements can be difficult because various body parts can move in many directions and often more than one joint is involved. For consistency, each joint is considered in isolation, three perpendicular planes are used for reference, and movements are described in relation to the anatomical position.
summary: Challenges in describing movement and the approach taken in the book.

content: The anatomical position is standing upright, feet parallel, arms hanging by the sides, palms and face directed forward.
summary: Definition of the anatomical position used as a reference point for describing movement.

content: Flexion of the wrist is a movement that takes the hand forward from the anatomical position. The median plane divides the body into symmetrical right and left halves. Any plane parallel to the median plane is called a sagittal plane. Movements in the sagittal plane can be seen from the side.
summary: Introduction to planes of motion and sagittal plane movements.

content: A movement in the sagittal plane that takes a body part forward from the anatomical position is called flexion. Examples of flexion include flexion of the hip and flexion of the shoulder. An exception to flexion is dorsiflexion of the ankle. A movement in the sagittal plane that takes a body part backward from the anatomical position is called extension. Examples of extension include extension of the neck and extension of the shoulder. Exceptions to extension include plantarflexion of the ankle and flexion of the knee.
summary: Specific examples of flexion and extension movements in the sagittal plane.

content: A coronal or frontal plane is any plane perpendicular to the median plane. A coronal or frontal plane divides the body into anterior and posterior parts. A movement in a frontal plane which takes a part of the body toward the median plane is called adduction. Movements on the frontal plane can be seen from the front. Example: adduction of the hip. For the trunk or neck, movement in the frontal plane away from the median plane is called lateral flexion or sidebending. Example: right lateral flexion of the trunk.
summary: Description of the coronal plane and movements within it.

content: A movement in a frontal plane which takes a part of the body toward the median plane is called adduction. Movements on the frontal plane can be seen from the front. Example: adduction of the hip. The opposite type of movement (away from the median plane) is called abduction. Example: abduction of the shoulder.
summary: This chunk defines adduction and abduction, movements in the frontal plane towards and away from the median plane respectively.

content: For the trunk or neck, movement in the frontal plane away from the median plane is called lateral flexion or sidebending. Example: right lateral flexion of the trunk.  For the fingers or toes, the reference used is the axis of the hand (middle finger) or foot (2d toe). Example: abduction of the fifth finger moves it away from the axis of the hand (not from the median plane).
summary: This chunk explains lateral flexion for the trunk and clarifies the reference point for abduction in the fingers and toes.

content: A transverse plane divides the body into superior and inferior (upper and lower) parts. Movements in this plane can be seen from the top or bottom. A movement in a transverse plane which takes a part of the body outward is called lateral rotation. Example: lateral rotation of the hip (the part being moved here is the front of the thigh). The opposite type of movement (inward) is called medial rotation. Example: medial rotation of the shoulder (the part being moved is the front of the upper arm).
summary: This chunk defines the transverse plane and explains lateral and medial rotation movements within it.

content: In supination of the forearm, the palm faces upward or forward. For the trunk or neck, we refer simply to right or left rotation. The reference point is the front of the chest or head. In pronation of the forearm, the palm of the hand faces downward or backward.
summary: This chunk describes supination, pronation, and rotation movements for the forearm and trunk.

content: Complex body movements typically involve movement in all three planes. Example: sitting in the 'tailor's position' involves flexion, abduction, and lateral rotation of the hip joints.
summary: This chunk explains that complex movements often involve multiple planes of motion.

content: Medial: closer to the median plane. Lateral: further from the median plane. Example: medial and lateral surfaces of the humerus. Anterior: facing toward or located at the front. Posterior: facing toward or located at the back. Example: anterior and posterior surfaces of the forearm. Superior: facing toward or located at the top (closer to the head). Inferior: facing toward or located at the bottom (further from the head). Example: superior and inferior ends of the femur.
summary: This chunk defines anatomical directional terms: medial, lateral, anterior, posterior, superior, and inferior.

content: Superficial (or external) on or near the outside (external) surface of the body or a particular bone or organ. Deep (or internal): inside the body or a particular bone or organ. Example: deep and superficial layer of the gastrocnemius muscle. Proximal: closer to the trunk, or some major joint. Distal: further from the trunk, or some major joint. Example: the articulations between bones of the fingers are called proximal and distal interphalangeal joints.
summary: This chunk defines anatomical terms related to depth and distance from the trunk: superficial, deep, proximal, and distal.

content: The bones act as levers and points of attachment for the muscles. Bone tissue consists of about two-thirds mineral components (mostly calcium salts), which give rigidity, and one-third organic components, which give elasticity. Bones are subjected to several types of mechanical strain: gravitational pressure from the body itself, movement (muscle contraction) against resistance, e.g., lifting a heavy object, and twisting forces.
summary: This chunk describes the structure and function of bones, highlighting their role as levers, points of attachment, and their resistance to mechanical strain.

content: The skeleton is a mobile framework of bones providing rigid support for the body.
summary: This chunk provides a concise definition of the skeleton's role in the body.

content: The skeleton is a mobile framework of bones providing rigid support for the body. Bones (particularly those of the feet, legs, and back) must support the weight of the body. Bones act as levers and points of attachment for the muscles. The bones also serve as levers for the action of muscles.
summary: The skeleton provides rigid support, bears body weight, and acts as levers for muscle action.

content: Bone tissue consists of about two-thirds mineral components (mostly calcium salts), which give rigidity, and one-third organic components, which give elasticity. Bones are subjected to several types of mechanical strain: gravitational pressure from the body itself, movement (muscle contraction) against resistance, e.g., lifting a heavy object, and twisting forces. Gravitational pressure from external objects (traction), e.g., supporting a heavy suitcase, is a type of mechanical strain bones are subjected to.
summary: Bone tissue is composed of mineral and organic components, and is subjected to various mechanical strains.

content: There are three basic shapes of bones: short bones such as the talus (ankle bone), flat bones such as the scapula, and long bones such as the radius and ulna.
summary: Bones come in three basic shapes: short, flat, and long.

content: A long bone, in this case the femur, consists of three parts: the central shaft called the diaphysis, and the two ends called the epiphyses. The diaphysis is a hollow tube with walls made of compact bone. The hollow structure of the diaphysis gives light weight and is actually sturdier than a solid structure would be. Compact bone is thickest in the middle section of the diaphysis where mechanical strains are greatest. Compact bone also predominates in the concave areas of the curved ends.
summary: Long bones have a diaphysis (shaft) and epiphyses (ends), with compact bone providing strength and a hollow structure for lightness.

content: A cross section of the epiphysis shows a trabecular (spongy) structure. Fibers are arranged in rows along the lines of greatest mechanical stress in the trabecular structure.
summary: The epiphyses of long bones have a spongy structure with fibers arranged to withstand stress.

content: The hollow part of the diaphysis contains the bone marrow. The marrow is red in children, but becomes yellow in adults as much as it is replaced by fatty tissue.
summary: The bone marrow, red in children and yellow in adults, is found within the diaphysis.

content: The external surface of the bone is covered with a membrane called the periosteum. The articular surfaces of the bones are covered with articular (hyaline) cartilage.
summary: Bones are covered by the periosteum and articular cartilage.

content: Joints are areas where bones are linked together. Joints have varying degrees of mobility. In some joints the bones are linked simply by fibrous connective tissue or cartilage, allowing little or no movement.
summary: Joints connect bones and vary in mobility, with some allowing little or no movement.

content: Our primary interest will be in freely-movable joints called diarthroses, or synovial joints. The surfaces (sometimes called facets) of synovial joints are shaped so as to fit together but also allow movement.
summary: Synovial joints are freely movable joints with shaped surfaces that allow movement.

content: There are many general categories of joints, based on the shape of the articulating surfaces. In a ball-and-socket joint, a ball-like surface of one bone fits into a cup-like depression of another. This type of joint, also called an enarthrodial or spheroidal joint, allows movement in all directions. Examples of ball-and-socket joints include the shoulder.
summary: Ball-and-socket joints, like the shoulder, allow movement in all directions.

content: In an ellipsoid joint, the shallow cavity of one bone receives the rounded surface of another. This allows movement in all three planes described on pages 8-10. Examples of ellipsoid joints include the metacarpophalangeal joint.
summary: Ellipsoid joints, like the metacarpophalangeal joint, allow movement in all three planes.

content: In a hinge joint, the convex surface of one bone fits against the concave surface of another. Movement is chiefly in one plane. Examples of hinge joints include the ankle.
summary: Hinge joints, like the ankle, allow movement primarily in one plane.

content: In a pivot joint, the rounded surface of one bone fits into a ring formed partly by another bone. Movement is chiefly in one direction, like the hinge of a door. Examples of pivot joints include the radioulnar joint.
summary: Pivot joints, like the radioulnar joint, allow movement primarily in one direction.

content: Both surfaces of a saddle are saddle-shaped, convex in one direction and concave in the other. This saddle-shaped structure permits movement in two planes.
summary: Saddle joints allow movement in two planes.

content: In a pivot joint, the rounded surface of one bone fits into a ring formed partly by another bone. Movement is chiefly in one direction, like the hinge of a door. Examples of pivot joints include the radioulnar joint.
summary: Pivot joints allow movement in one direction, like a door hinge, with examples including the radioulnar joint.

content: Both surfaces of a saddle are saddle-shaped, convex in one direction and concave in the other. This saddle-shaped structure permits movement in two planes.
summary: Saddle joints have a saddle-shaped structure, allowing movement in two planes.

content: Some joints are more stable than others. The ball-and-socket structure of the hip is deep and snug-fitting. The ball-and-socket of the shoulder is shallow, looser, and less stable.
summary: Joint stability varies; the hip is more stable than the shoulder due to its deeper socket.

content: Between the articulating ends of the two bones in a joint is a gap which is not opaque to X-rays. This gap corresponds to the articular cartilages and synovial cavity. When you look at an X-ray, you can only see the thickness of the cartilages in the joint. The cartilages themselves are not visible on X-ray. Thus, it looks like there is a free space between the two bones.
summary: X-rays reveal the thickness of joint cartilage but not the cartilage itself, creating the appearance of a gap between bones.

content: In a dislocation or subluxation, a bone is moved from its normal position in a joint because of some trauma. There is associated damage to ligaments, etc.
summary: Dislocations and subluxations involve bone displacement from the joint, often accompanied by ligament damage.

content: Articulating surfaces of bones are covered with a shiny, whitish connective tissue called cartilage. Cartilage contributes to the synovial capsule and also protects the underlying bone tissue. Cartilage is well-adapted to stresses, being strong, resilient, and smooth. Thus, it can allow some sliding of the bones relative to each other.
summary: Cartilage covers articulating bone surfaces, protecting bone and allowing smooth movement.

content: When movement occurs, joint cartilage is subjected to two types of stress: gravitational pressure (particularly in the weight-bearing joints of the legs and feet) and friction from the movement itself. Nevertheless, cartilage may be damaged either by trauma or excessive wear (e.g., when the ends of the articulating bones do not provide a good 'fit').
summary: Joint cartilage experiences stress from both gravity and movement, and can be damaged by trauma or poor joint fit.

content: Rheumatoid arthritis and osteoarthritis are two common diseases involving damage to joint cartilage, accompanied by inflammation, pain, and stiffness of the joint and surrounding muscles.
summary: Rheumatoid and osteoarthritis are diseases characterized by joint cartilage damage, inflammation, pain, and stiffness.

content: Joint cartilage (like all cartilage) does not contain blood vessels. It receives nutrients from the synovial fluid and the bone it covers.
summary: Joint cartilage lacks blood vessels and relies on synovial fluid and bone for nutrient supply.

content: Fibrocartilage contains high concentrations of collagenous (white) fibers and is specially adapted for absorbing shock. Fibrocartilage is found in the intervertebral discs and in the menisci (articular discs) of the knee and other large joints.
summary: Fibrocartilage, rich in collagen, absorbs shock and is found in intervertebral discs and knee menisci.

content: The joint capsule encloses the joint, prevents loss of fluid, and binds together the ends of the articulating bones. The outer layer of the capsule is composed of dense connective tissue and represents a continuation of the periosteum.
summary: The joint capsule encloses the joint, prevents fluid loss, and binds articulating bones.

content: The joint capsule is stronger where movement must be prevented. Fibers of the outer capsule are often arranged in parallel bundles (called ligaments; see following section) to reinforce joints and prevent unwanted movement. The capsule may be arranged loosely or in folds where movement is possible.
summary: Joint capsule strength varies depending on the need for stability, with ligaments reinforcing joints and allowing for flexibility in other areas.

content: The interior of the capsule is covered by a synovial membrane, which covers the deep surface of the capsule and folds over at the capsular insertions. Its principal function is to secrete synovial fluid that fills the articular cavity. This fluid lubricates the joint and provides nutrients to the cartilage.
summary: The synovial membrane secretes synovial fluid, which lubricates the joint and nourishes cartilage.

content: Ligaments are dense bundles of parallel collagenous fibers. Ligaments are often derived from the outer layer of the joint capsule, but may also connect nearby but non-articulating bones. The ligaments connecting the sacrum to the spine and tuberosity of the ischium is an example of a ligament.
summary: Ligaments are dense collagenous fibers connecting bones, often originating from the joint capsule.

content: Ligaments function chiefly to strengthen and stabilize the joint in a passive way. Unlike muscles, ligaments cannot actively contract.
summary: Ligaments passively stabilize and strengthen joints, unlike muscles which can contract.

content: Ligaments are dense bundles of parallel collagenous fibers. Ligaments are often derived from the outer layer of the joint capsule, but may also connect nearby but non-articulating bones. The ligaments connecting the sacrum to the spine and tuberosity of the ischium is an example of a ligament. Ligaments function chiefly to strengthen and stabilize the joint in a passive way. Unlike muscles, ligaments cannot actively contract. Except for a few ligaments which contain a high proportion of yellow elastic fibers, ligaments also cannot stretch. The fibular (lateral) collateral ligament of the knee is pulled tight in extension and loosened in flexion.
summary: Ligaments are connective tissues that provide passive stability to joints, primarily composed of collagen fibers and lacking the ability to contract or stretch significantly.

content: Ligaments contain numerous sensory nerve cells capable of responding to the speed, movement, and position of the joint, as well as to stretching or pain. Sensory cells in ligaments constantly transmit information to the brain, which in turn sends signals to the muscles via motor neurons. This is called the proprioceptive sense. Excessive movement of the joint can lead to ligamentous stretching to the point of straining or tearing the ligament. This is called a sprain.
summary: Ligaments possess sensory receptors that provide proprioceptive feedback about joint position and movement, and excessive strain can result in ligament sprains.

content: Essentially all movements of the human body result from contraction of muscles. This book focuses on skeletal muscles (also known as voluntary or striated muscles) which attach to bones. In a microscopic cross section, a muscle is composed of bundles of fibers (primary, secondary, tertiary), held together and compartmentalized by fibrous partitions called deep fascia, epimysium, perimysium, and endomysium. They can be extended beyond the muscle to form a strong fibrous cord called a tendon which is continuous with the periosteum of a nearby bone, and serves to attach the muscle to the bone. A broad, flattened tendon is called an aponeurosis. These connective tissue partitions (which are continuous with each other) allow easy movement of one muscle or muscle group relative to another.
summary: Skeletal muscles, responsible for body movements, are composed of bundles of fibers attached to bones via tendons, allowing for coordinated muscle actions.

content: Each muscle cell, also called a muscle fiber, contains extremely long myofibrils. Each myofibril contains a contractile element in its central part, the sarcomere. The structure of the sarcomere appears (under extreme magnification) to consist of filaments: dark bundles, thick filaments, which are rounded in the middle (consisting of myosin, a type of protein), and light bundles, thin filaments, linked to each other through their central part, consisting of actin (another type of protein). At rest, the actin and myosin filaments are not connected. When the muscle contracts, they unite, and pull each other. This causes a thickening (diameter) and shortening (length), which makes it possible for the muscle to pull the bones to which it is attached.
summary: Muscle contraction occurs at the sarcomere level, where actin and myosin filaments interact, resulting in muscle shortening and force generation.

content: Typically, a muscle is attached to two different bones. For a given body movement, one bone (called the origin) is fixed in some way, and the other (called the insertion) moves as a result of muscle contraction. The origin is often the proximal bone, and the insertion the distal bone, but there are many exceptions. For example, the gluteus medius connects the ilium (large hip bone) to the femur. If the ilium is fixed, contraction of this muscle results in abduction of the femur. If one is standing with the weight on the leg, the femur becomes the fixed point, and contraction of the muscle results in lateral flexion of the pelvis.
summary: Muscles exert force by contracting, pulling on bones and causing movement. The origin and insertion points of a muscle determine its action.

content: In this book, we will describe muscle actions where the proximal attachment is the fixed point. For some muscles or regions, we will add the muscle action where the distal attachment is the fixed point. Besides their (active) ability to contract, muscles have a (passive) property of elasticity. When stretched, they tend to return to their normal resting length. For example, the anterior neck muscles, when they contract, are flexors of the neck. During extension of the neck, these muscles become stretched. When this happens, because of their elasticity, they tend to return the head to its anatomical position.
summary: Muscles exhibit both active (contraction) and passive (elasticity) properties, influencing movement and joint stability.

content: Muscles attach to bones in several ways: directly via muscle fibers (usually in a broad insertion), via an aponeurosis (broad tendon), via a regular tendon, and via the tendon passing under a fibrous band. Some muscles have several origins (called "heads"), which may be on more than one bone. Example: biceps brachii has two heads, triceps brachii has three heads, and quadriceps femoris has four heads.
summary: Muscles connect to bones through various attachment methods, including direct insertions, tendons, and aponeuroses. Some muscles have multiple origins.

content: During extension of the neck, these muscles become stretched. When this happens, because of their elasticity, they tend to return the head to its anatomical position.
summary: Muscles involved in neck extension are stretched during extension and return the head to its neutral position due to elasticity.

content: Muscles attach to bones in several ways: directly via muscle fibers (usually in a broad insertion), via an aponeurosis (broad tendon), via a regular tendon, and via the tendon passing under a fibrous band. Some muscles have several origins (called "heads"), which may be on more than one bone. Example: biceps brachii has two heads, triceps brachii has three heads, and quadriceps femoris has four heads. Typically, the proximal insertion of the muscle is called origin and the distal one is called insertion. Example: the psoas muscle has its origin on the vertebrae and its insertion on the femur. A muscle can have several origins, Example: flexor digitorum superficialis originates from both the radius and ulna, and several insertions. Example: the interosseous muscles insert on the phalanges and extensor tendons of fingers.
summary: Muscles attach to bones through various methods, including direct insertions, tendons, and aponeuroses. Some muscles have multiple origins and insertions.

content: The fiber bundles of the trapezius muscle are arranged in a flattened and fan-shaped manner. The deep back muscles are short and appear as small bundles along the vertebral column. The biceps brachii muscle is shaped like a long spindle.
summary: Muscle shapes and fiber arrangements vary, with examples including the flattened trapezius, short deep back muscles, and spindle-shaped biceps brachii.

content: Muscles may act in one or several directions depending on the orientation and attachment of their fibers. The fibers of the rectus abdominis muscle run essentially parallel to each other and flex the trunk. The fibers of the external oblique muscle are arranged like a fan and can produce anterior flexion, sidebending, or rotation of the trunk.
summary: Muscle action direction depends on fiber orientation. Rectus abdominis fibers flex the trunk, while external oblique fibers allow for multiple trunk movements.

content: Long muscles are usually kinetic, meaning they are able to produce highly visible external motion. Short, deep muscles tend to be responsible for precise, small-scale adjustments rather than gross movements.
summary: Long muscles produce visible movements, while short, deep muscles facilitate precise, subtle adjustments.

content: A muscle which crosses and affects a single joint is called monoarticular. A muscle which crosses (and moves) more than one joint is called polyarticular.
summary: Muscles can be classified as monoarticular (affecting one joint) or polyarticular (affecting multiple joints).

content: To stretch a muscle, you move it in a manner that is the opposite of its usual action around each of its joints. The rectus femoris muscle crosses the hip and knee joints and is both a flexor of the hip and an extensor of the knee. The rectus femoris muscle will be stretched in situations involving simultaneous extension of the hip and flexion of the knee.
summary: Stretching involves opposing a muscle's usual action. The rectus femoris muscle, crossing both hip and knee joints, is stretched by simultaneous hip extension and knee flexion.

content: The muscle which produces a particular movement is called the agonist, and the muscle which produces the opposite movement is called the antagonist. Mutually opposing muscles often function together to fix or stabilize a bone.
summary: Agonists produce movement, while antagonists oppose it. Opposing muscles often work together for stability.

content: Different muscles which cooperate to produce the same action are called synergetic. Three muscles work synergetically in dorsiflexion of the ankle: tibialis anterior, extensor hallucis longus, and extensor digitorum longus. In hip flexion, the psoas major is the agonist and the gluteus maximus (a hip extensor) is the antagonist.
summary: Synergistic muscles work together for a common action. Examples include dorsiflexion muscles and hip flexion/extension.

content: The serratus anterior and rhomboids have opposite actions; they protract and retract the scapula, respectively, i.e., move it away from and toward the vertebral column. By contracting at the same time, the serratus anterior and rhomboids work together to fix the scapula.
summary: Serratus anterior and rhomboids have opposing scapular actions, but can work together for scapular stabilization.

content: When a muscle contracts, it tends to draw its origin and insertion points closer together. Anything that opposes this tendency is called resistance. The brachialis and biceps brachii are the major flexors of the elbow. Resistance to the action of the brachialis and biceps brachii can be provided by the weight of the forearm, the weight of an external object attached to the forearm, the force of another person pulling on the arm, or the contraction of antagonist muscles (in this case the triceps brachii, the major elbow extensor).
summary: Muscle contraction draws origin and insertion points closer, with resistance opposing this action. Examples include elbow flexion with various resistance sources.

content: When a movement occurs because of an active muscle, the muscle insertions are moved closer together. This type of contraction is called concentric. In the supine position, a concentric contraction of the trunk flexors occurs. Two people (A and B) pulling each other (elbows are flexed) results in A winning, which is a concentric contraction of A's elbow flexors.
summary: Concentric muscle contractions involve shortening as movement occurs. Examples include trunk flexion and elbow flexion during a pull.

content: In some cases, a muscle works without initiating the action itself; instead, it 'applies the brakes' to the action.
summary: Some muscles act as brakes, controlling or resisting movement rather than initiating it.

content: In a standing position, the rectus abdominis is not active, and gravity causes the trunk to fall forward. In the supine position, a concentric contraction of the trunk flexors occurs. In the standing position, the trunk flexors do not initiate flexion; gravity does. If the muscles didn't work, flexion would consist of a 'falling' forward. To slow down flexion, the extensors of the trunk must contract in order to restrain or 'brake' this flexion.
summary: Describes the role of trunk muscles in maintaining posture and controlling flexion.

content: When a movement occurs because of an active muscle, the muscle insertions are moved closer together. This type of contraction is called concentric. Two people (A and B) pulling each other (elbows are flexed) results in A winning, which is a concentric contraction of A's elbow flexors. When A 'loses' and brakes the pull of B, an eccentric contraction of A's elbow flexors occurs.
summary: Explains concentric and eccentric muscle contractions using examples of pulling.

content: In some cases, a muscle works without initiating the action itself; instead, it 'applies the brakes' to the action. Without the work of this muscle braking the action, it would occur faster. If the muscles which oppose a movement apply the brakes, their contraction is called eccentric. This occurs during lengthening of the muscles.
summary: Defines eccentric muscle contractions as 'braking' actions and their role in controlling movement speed.

content: When the rectus femoris and hamstring muscles combine their actions by flexing the hip and knee (e.g., when squatting or doing a 'grand plie'), the bones involved change their position, but the muscles do not lengthen or shorten. The hip and knees 'cancel out' each other's actions.
summary: Describes how hip and knee flexion can occur without muscle lengthening or shortening.

content: It is possible for a muscle to be contracted even without movement. The thigh can be flexed (concentric contraction of the hip flexors) and then held in this position. When a position is fixed by a muscular contraction, we call this contraction isometric. During an isometric contraction, the insertions of the muscles do not move.
summary: Explains isometric muscle contractions, where muscle tension is maintained without movement.

content: If we apply the illustration above to the knee, the following would happen when stretching the knee: isometric contraction at the hip flexors and isotonic (concentric) contraction of the knee extensors.
summary: Illustrates combined muscle contractions during a knee stretch.

content: The trunk is the central part of the body. This book will examine only the locomotor functions of the trunk, not its internal organs. The trunk serves a double function, which is connected to its bony structure, the vertebral column. The trunk can bend and perform curved movements, like those of a serpent or a measuring tape.
summary: Introduces the trunk and its role in locomotion, focusing on its bony structure and movement capabilities.

content: The mobility of the trunk is due to the flexibility of the vertebral column, which has twenty-six 'levels' of articulation. The vertebral column contains a tunnel for the nerves: the spinal cord and the nerve roots, which exit the spinal cord. Weakness in the vertebrae may therefore affect not only the joints, but also the spinal cord and nerves.
summary: Describes the vertebral column's structure and its importance for trunk mobility and nerve protection.

content: The trunk must be able to align and stabilize the vertebral segments when the body is motionless, and especially when it is carrying a load. This dual function depends on a finely integrated system of mostly polyarticular muscles, which are either deep (composed of numerous small bundles) or superficial (usually arranged like broad sheets). Movements of the pelvis are difficult to separate from those of the vertebral column.
summary: Explains the trunk's role in stability and its reliance on a complex muscle system.

content: The trunk can move anteriorly (flexion), laterally (lateral flexion or sidebending), and on its own axis (rotation). Range of movement of the trunk varies depending on vertebral level. The shape of the vertebrae, thickness of intervertebral discs, and articulation of thoracic vertebrae with ribs influence trunk movement. The trunk can move posteriorly (extension).
summary: Lists the different types of trunk movements and factors influencing their range.

content: Do not confuse trunk movements with those in which the trunk moves as a single unit at the hip joint. Trunk movement can be a secondary consequence of limb movement. Abduction of the arm takes the trunk into lateral flexion. Front-to-back or side-to-side movements of the head and pelvis while using a hula-hoop involve trunk movement.
summary: Differentiates trunk movements from movements at the hip joint and provides examples of how limb movements can influence trunk motion.

content: Often, two or more trunk movements are performed in combination. Examples of combined trunk movements include rotation, extension, and lateral flexion. The vertebral column (or spine) forms a mobile bony stem that is part of the trunk skeleton.
summary: Highlights the common occurrence of combined trunk movements and defines the vertebral column's role in the trunk.

content: The vertebral column (or spine) forms a mobile bony stem that is part of the trunk skeleton. The spine consists of the cervical, thoracic, lumbar, sacrum, and coccyx regions. There are seven cervical vertebrae, twelve thoracic vertebrae, five lumbar vertebrae, the sacrum, and the coccyx. Vertebrae are numbered sequentially from top to bottom and are referred to by a letter plus a number.
summary: The vertebral column, or spine, is composed of distinct regions (cervical, thoracic, lumbar, sacrum, coccyx) with a specific number of vertebrae in each.

content: The exact form of vertebral column curvatures varies from person to person. Kyphosis is almost non-existent in some individuals. The external appearance of vertebral column curvatures can be affected by overlying soft structures. A person with large buttocks may appear to have more pronounced lordosis than a person with small buttocks. X-rays could reveal identical lumbar curvatures.
summary: Individual vertebral column curvatures vary, and external appearance can be influenced by soft tissues, while X-rays provide a more accurate representation.

content: There are several characteristic curvatures of the vertebral column: the sacrum is convex toward the back, the lumbar region is concave, the thoracic region is convex, and the cervical region is concave.
summary: The vertebral column exhibits distinct curvatures in each region (sacrum, lumbar, thoracic, cervical), contributing to its overall flexibility and function.

content: Each vertebra consists of two main parts: the massive body (anterior) and the vertebral arch (posterior). The shape and size of a vertebra vary depending on its position in the spine. The vertebral arch can be divided into many parts and is connected to the body by two pedicles. Two lamina unite posteriorly to form a spinous process.
summary: Each vertebra comprises a body and an arch, with variations in shape and structure depending on its location within the spine.

content: The vertebral body is roughly cylindrical and consists of six surfaces. The opening between the body and the arch is called the vertebral foramen. The foramina of many vertebrae lined up form the vertebral canal through which the spinal cord passes.
summary: The vertebral body has a distinct shape and houses the vertebral foramen, which collectively form the vertebral canal protecting the spinal cord.

content: The thickened junctions between the pedicles and laminae have superior and inferior cartilaginous articular facets and a laterally-projecting transverse process. The vertebral holes stacked on top of each other form a bony pipe called the spinal canal, through which the spinal cord passes.
summary: Vertebrae possess articular facets and transverse processes, contributing to their articulation and stability within the spinal canal.

content: The spaces between the pedicles of adjacent vertebrae form a series of openings called intervertebral foramina. As spinal nerves branch off the spinal cord, they exit through the intervertebral foramina.
summary: Intervertebral foramina provide passage for spinal nerves as they branch from the spinal cord.

content: Each vertebra is attached to its neighbor by three joints (except for the atlas-axis joint). Anteriorly, the bodies are joined by the fibrocartilaginous intervertebral disc. Posteriorly, between the articular processes of the vertebrae are the zygapophyseal joints.
summary: Vertebrae are interconnected by three types of joints: intervertebral discs, zygapophyseal joints, and the atlas-axis joint.

content: The two inferior articular facets of the top vertebra contact the two superior articular facets of the bottom vertebra. The articular facets are small and mainly serve to guide movements. The facets are covered with cartilage and held together by a capsule and a number of small ligaments.
summary: Articular facets on adjacent vertebrae guide movement and are stabilized by cartilage and ligaments.

content: The intervertebral disc contains two distinct types of material: the annulus fibrosus and the nucleus pulposus. The annulus fibrosus is composed of concentric rings of fibrocartilage arranged like the layers of an onion. The nucleus pulposus is made of a gelatinous substance.
summary: The intervertebral disc consists of the annulus fibrosus (fibrocartilage rings) and the nucleus pulposus (gelatinous substance), providing cushioning and flexibility.

content: The disc allows movement between vertebrae, acts as a shock absorber, and bears weight.
summary: The intervertebral disc facilitates movement, absorbs shock, and supports weight distribution.

content: There are three ligaments extending the length of the vertebral column. The supraspinous ligament runs along the tips of the spinous processes and acts as a brake to flexion. The posterior longitudinal ligament is attached to the back of the vertebral bodies and absorbs the thrust from the disc nuclei in flexion.
summary: Three ligaments (supraspinous, posterior longitudinal, anterior longitudinal) provide stability and control movement along the vertebral column.

content: The anterior longitudinal ligament is attached to the front of the vertebral bodies and acts as a brake to extension.
summary: The anterior longitudinal ligament restricts excessive extension of the spine.

content: Abduction of the arm takes the trunk into lateral flexion. Front-to-back or side-to-side movements of the head and pelvis while using a hula-hoop involve trunk movement. Often, two or more trunk movements are performed in combination. Examples of combined trunk movements include rotation, extension, and lateral flexion.
summary: Trunk movement encompasses a variety of actions, including lateral flexion, rotation, extension, and combinations thereof.

content: The vertebral column is attached to the base of the cranium, the ribs, and the pelvis (ilium).
summary: The vertebral column is structurally connected to the cranium, ribs, and pelvis.

content: The disc allows movement between vertebrae, acts as a shock absorber, and bears weight. The disc often receives pressure from the vertebral body above. The nucleus of the intervertebral disc distributes pressure it receives in every direction due to its central location and gelatinous composition. The intervertebral disc serves as a shock absorber.
summary: The intervertebral disc facilitates movement, absorbs shock, and distributes weight, acting as a crucial shock absorber in the spine.

content: There are three ligaments extending the length of the vertebral column. The supraspinous ligament runs along the tips of the spinous processes and acts as a brake to flexion. The posterior longitudinal ligament is attached to the back of the vertebral bodies and absorbs the thrust from the disc nuclei in flexion. The anterior longitudinal ligament is attached to the front of the vertebral bodies and acts as a brake to extension.
summary: The vertebral column is supported by three primary ligaments: the supraspinous, posterior longitudinal, and anterior longitudinal ligaments, each playing a role in limiting specific spinal movements.

content: The other ligaments are discontinuous and hold the posterior arches of the individual vertebrae together. Interspinous and intertransverse ligaments connect the spinous and transverse processes. The surfaces of the articular processes are linked together through a capsule insetted on its circumference. The inside of the capsule is reinforced by an extension of the ligamentum flavum and, at the back, by a posterior ligament. These ligaments are elastic and can be pierced by a syringe during a spinal tap.
summary: Additional ligaments connect individual vertebrae, providing stability and allowing for controlled movement. These ligaments, including the ligamentum flavum, are elastic and can be accessed during medical procedures.

content: We can think of the vertebral column as a series of fixed segments (the vertebrae) having mobile connections (discs, ligaments). Movements of individual vertebrae are compounded such that the entire structure has considerable mobility in three dimensions. Type and extent of mobility varies with different spinal regions, depending on size and shape of vertebrae, and other factors.
summary: The vertebral column's structure allows for both stability and flexibility. Individual vertebrae are connected by mobile discs and ligaments, enabling a wide range of movements.

content: In flexion, the top vertebra (B) tilts toward the front. The superior articular facets slide on the inferior ones in flexion. The disc is compressed anteriorly and expanded posteriorly in flexion. The various parts of the vertebral arches are pulled apart in flexion, and the ligaments connecting these parts are stretched in flexion.
summary: During flexion, the spine bends forward, with the top vertebra tilting, discs compressing anteriorly, and ligaments stretching.

content: In extension, the opposite occurs. B tilts toward the back. The disc is compressed posteriorly and expanded anteriorly in extension. The nucleus of the disc moves forward in extension. The articular facets are pressed together in extension. The arches move closer together in extension. The posterior ligaments are relaxed in extension. The anterior longitudinal ligament is stretched in extension.
summary: Extension involves the opposite movements of flexion, with the spine bending backward, discs compressing posteriorly, and ligaments stretching.

content: Lateral flexion involves the left sides of vertebrae A and B moving closer together. On the left side of the spine during lateral flexion, the transverse processes and articular facets come closer together, and the associated ligaments relax. During left lateral flexion, the right side of the spine moves farther apart. The disc expands to the right side during left lateral flexion, and its nucleus moves to the right. The opposite movements occur on the right side during right lateral flexion.
summary: Lateral flexion involves bending the spine to one side, with the associated ligaments relaxing and the disc expanding on the convex side.

content: In rotation, the fibers of the intervertebral disc are under torsion (twisting). The fibers of the intervertebral disc alternate in orientation from one layer to the next. One layer of the intervertebral disc is stretched while the next layer is relaxed during rotation. Rotation diminishes the distance between the vertebrae. The nucleus of the intervertebral disc is slightly compressed during rotation. All connecting ligaments are stretched by rotation.
summary: Rotation involves twisting movements, with the intervertebral disc fibers experiencing torsion and ligaments stretching.

content: The nucleus of the intervertebral disc is slightly compressed during rotation. All connecting ligaments are stretched by rotation. The intervertebral disc serves as a shock absorber. The disc often receives pressure from the vertebral body above. The nucleus of the intervertebral disc distributes pressure it receives in every direction due to its central location and gelatinous composition. The fibers of the annulus receive both horizontal and vertical pressures. As long as fluid remains in the nucleus, the disc performs its role as a shock absorber very efficiently. The disc may partially lose its shock-absorbing property due to the aging process and/or excessive wear and tear. Cracks develop in the annulus through which the fluid of the nucleus can escape. A herniated or ruptured disc occurs when the fluid of the nucleus escapes.
summary: The intervertebral disc functions as a shock absorber, distributing pressure and being affected by rotation.  Disc degeneration can lead to herniation.

content: A herniated disc happens most commonly as a result of chronic flexion movements. During chronic flexion movements, the nucleus moves toward the back, and fluid can escape. Fluid from a herniated disc may compress nerve roots, such as the sciatic nerve. Chronic or sudden extreme tension on the posterior longitudinal ligament can result in chronic lumbar backaches. It is important to avoid 'loaded' vertebral flexion, such as flexing the lumbar spine while lifting a heavy object. It is preferable to avoid loaded lumbar flexions in any type of physical exercise, even if you are not lifting any object. Keep the spine straight and flex at the hip and knee joints only.
summary: Chronic flexion movements can lead to herniated discs and back pain. Proper lifting technique and avoiding excessive lumbar flexion are crucial.

content: The pelvis (or pelvic girdle) is a ring-shaped structure. The pelvis consists of four main bones: the sacrum, two hipbones, and the coccyx. The pelvis receives the weight of the upper body and passes this weight on to the lower limbs via its articulations with the femurs. The pelvis must absorb stresses from the lower limbs, such as in walking or jumping. The shape of the bones forms a greater (false) pelvis at the top and a lesser (true) pelvis at the bottom. The top opening of the lesser pelvis is called the pelvic inlet.
summary: The pelvis is a ring-shaped structure that supports the upper body and transmits weight to the lower limbs, absorbing stresses from movement.

content: The hip bones are flat and consist of a superior and inferior part, which are twisted against each other. In the adult, each hip consists of three bones that are fused together: the ilium, ischium, and pubis. These bones fuse at a cartilage in the shape of a Y, located in the center of the socket. The hip has two surfaces (medial and lateral) and four edges (superior, inferior, anterior, and posterior). On the lateral surface of the hip bone, we find: • in the middle is an area in the form of a hollow sphere, called the acetabulum, which receives the head of the femur. The superior border or iliac crest is located on the lateral surface of the hip bone. The lower part of the lateral surface of the hip bone is like a bony arch, which surrounds a hole called the obturator foramen. The anterior area of the lateral surface of the hip bone is the pubis, and the posterior area is the ischium. The ischio-pubic ramus is located between the ischium and pubis.
summary: The hip bone is a complex structure composed of fused bones, forming the socket for the femur and featuring various landmarks.

content: The anterior border of the hip bone has a number of depressions and protuberances, especially: the anterior superior iliac spine, the most forward part of the iliac crest, the anterior inferior iliac spine, and the pubic tubercle. The top part of the anterior border of the hip bone, which is concave from bottom to top, is the external iliac fossa. Seen from a three-quarter posterior view, we find the posterior border of the hip bone, which shows various protuberances and depressions, notably: the ischial tuberosity, lesser sciatic notch, ischial spine, greater sciatic notch, posterior inferior iliac spine, and posterior superior iliac spine. The ischial tuberosity is the bone on which you sit.
summary: The hip bone has distinct anterior and posterior borders with various prominences and depressions, including the ischial tuberosity, which serves as the sitting surface.

content: The medial surface of the hip bones of the pelvis contains the internal iliac fossa. The medial surface of the hip bones of the pelvis contains an oblique crest. The iliopectineal line, located on the medial surface of the hip bones of the pelvis, forms the border between the lesser and greater pelvis. The medial surface of the hip bones of the pelvis contains the internal circumference of the obturator foramen.
summary: The medial surface of the hip bone features the internal iliac fossa, an oblique crest, and the iliopectineal line, which separates the lesser and greater pelvis.

content: The ischial tuberosity is the bone on which you sit. The shape and proportions of the pelvis vary considerably in normal individuals. The gap between the two ischial tuberosities may be bigger or smaller. Lack of 'padding' by muscles and adipose tissue, combined with the protrusion of the sacral crest or posterior superior iliac spines, can result in difficulty doing floor exercises, particularly rolling on a hard surface.
summary: The ischial tuberosity is the sitting bone, and pelvic shape variations can affect floor exercise performance.

content: The medial surface of the hip bones of the pelvis contains the internal iliac fossa. The medial surface of the hip bones of the pelvis contains an oblique crest. The iliopectineal line, located on the medial surface of the hip bones of the pelvis, forms the border between the lesser and greater pelvis. The medial surface of the hip bones of the pelvis contains the internal circumference of the obturator foramen.
summary: The medial surface of the hip bones contains several key features, including the iliac fossa, oblique crest, iliopectineal line, and obturator foramen.

content: The medial surface of the hip bones of the pelvis contains an articular surface located in front of the pubis, in the shape of an oval covered by cartilage. The pubic symphyseal surface is the articular surface located in front of the pubis, in the shape of an oval covered by cartilage, which unites the two pubic bones. The articulation between the two pubic bones is called the pubic symphysis. Between the two surfaces of the pubic symphysis is a fibrocartilage disc which attaches to the articular surfaces.
summary: The pubic symphysis is the joint connecting the two pubic bones, featuring a fibrocartilage disc and articular surfaces.

content: The pubic symphysis is a joint with very little mobility. Only small gliding, compression, and twisting movements are possible at the pubic symphysis. The pubic symphysis loosens during childbirth so that the pelvis can open up further.
summary: The pubic symphysis is a relatively immobile joint that allows for slight movement and widens during childbirth.

content: The entire pubic symphysis structure is covered by a fibrous cuff, reinforced by four ligaments: anterior, posterior, superior, and inferior.
summary: The pubic symphysis is reinforced by a fibrous cuff and four ligaments.

content: There are obvious differences in pelvic shape between males and females. The male pelvis is narrower. The female pelvis is wider, with a larger pelvic inlet and outlet. The differences in pelvic shape between males and females are all related to the ability of women to carry and deliver a child.
summary: Male and female pelvises differ in shape, with females having a wider pelvis to accommodate childbirth.

content: The pelvis is sometimes also called the pelvic girdle. Anatomically, girdles are bony and articular structures by which the limbs are attached to the trunk. The shoulder girdle, located at the top of the trunk, consists of the sternum, the two clavicles, and the two scapulae. The shoulder girdle attaches the upper limbs to the trunk. The shoulder girdle is characterized by its mobility. The shoulder girdle is not linked via joints to the spinal column, but instead is linked to the thoracic cage.
summary: The pelvis is a type of girdle, a bony structure connecting limbs to the trunk, similar to the shoulder girdle.

content: The pelvic girdle, or pelvis, is located at the bottom of the trunk and consists of the sacrum and the two hip bones. The pelvic girdle attaches the lower limbs to the trunk. The pelvic girdle is a very stable structure because the articulations between its bones are not very mobile. The pelvic girdle is attached to the trunk via the sacro-lumbar joint, which connects it to the spinal column.
summary: The pelvic girdle, composed of the sacrum and hip bones, is a stable structure connecting the lower limbs to the trunk.

content: The sacral promontory is the upper, anterior edge of S1. The sacral promontory, together with the iliopectineal lines, defines the boundary between the greater and lesser pelvis. The anterior surface of the sacrum is smooth and concave. There are four transverse ridges on the anterior surface of the sacrum, which represent intervertebral discs.
summary: The sacral promontory, located on the upper anterior edge of S1, defines the boundary between the greater and lesser pelvis.

content: The superior surface of S1, called the base of the sacrum, articulates with L5. The inferior surface of S5, which articulates with the coccyx, is called the apex of the sacrum. At the ends of each ridge on the sacrum are paired anterior sacral foramina (corresponding to intervertebral foramina), through which the anterior branches of the sacral nerves pass.
summary: The sacrum has a base (articulating with L5) and an apex (articulating with the coccyx), with foramina for sacral nerves.

content: The sacrum is the posterior, wedge-shaped component of the pelvic ring, located between the two ilia. The sacrum is composed of five expanded, fused vertebrae (S1-S5) whose individual components are readily visible. The superior surface of S1, called the base of the sacrum, articulates with L5. The inferior surface of S5, which articulates with the coccyx, is called the apex of the sacrum.
summary: The sacrum is a wedge-shaped bone formed from fused vertebrae, articulating with the lumbar spine and coccyx.

content: The anterior surface of the sacrum is smooth and concave. There are four transverse ridges on the anterior surface of the sacrum, which represent intervertebral discs. At the ends of each ridge on the sacrum are paired anterior sacral foramina (corresponding to intervertebral foramina), through which the anterior branches of the sacral nerves pass.
summary: The sacrum's anterior surface features ridges and foramina that transmit nerves.

content: The convex posterior surface of the coccyx is much rougher. The coccyx is a small triangular bone consisting of the fusion of three to five vertebrae. The coccyx articulates with the sacrum via an oval-shaped surface. The coccyx is supported by a capsule and ligaments (this joint is often fused).
summary: The coccyx is a fused triangular bone that articulates with the sacrum.

content: The sacral canal is located within the sacrum and the spinal cord enters through it. The median sacral crest and paired lateral sacral crests represent the spinous and transverse processes of the vertebrae.
summary: The sacrum houses the sacral canal, through which the spinal cord passes.

content: The auricular ('ear-shaped') surfaces of the sacrum are best seen in side view. The auricular surfaces of the sacrum articulate with the auricular surfaces of the ilia. The auricular surface of the sacrum is slightly concave. The auricular surface of the hip bone is slightly convex.
summary: The sacrum and ilium articulate via auricular surfaces, forming the sacroiliac joint.

content: The sacroiliac joint consists of the two auricular surfaces on top of the ilium and the sacrum. The arrangement of the auricular surfaces allows for an important movement between the sacrum and the two ilia, called nutation and counternutation.
summary: The sacroiliac joint permits nutation and counternutation, movements of the sacrum relative to the ilia.

content: In nutation, the sacral base tilts anteroinferiorly, and the sacral apex tilts postefosuperiorly. During nutation, the sacral promontory moves toward the pubis, and the sacral apex moves away from the pubis, while the ischial bones move away from each other, increasing the distance between them. In one type of movement, called 'adduction' of the pelvis, the sacrum goes into nutation, the iliac 'wings' are pulled medially, and the ischial tuberosities move laterally.
summary: Nutation involves tilting of the sacrum and associated pelvic movements.

content: During adduction, the diameter of the pelvic outlet increases and the pelvic inlet decreases from front to back, especially in the lower portion. In the movement called 'abduction' of the pelvis, the sacrum moves in counternutation as the sacral base tilts backward and upward and the sacral apex tilts forward and downward. During abduction, the iliac wings move away from the midline and the ischial bones move toward each other.
summary: Abduction and adduction of the pelvis involve sacral counternutation and nutation, respectively, altering pelvic dimensions.

content: During abduction, the pelvic inlet becomes larger from front to back, and the diameter of the pelvic outlet becomes smaller. Dimensional changes between the pelvic inlet and outlet occur especially during childbirth. At the beginning of the baby's engagement in the pelvis, there will be a sacral counternutation, and when the baby comes out (expulsion), a nutation.
summary: Pelvic movements during childbirth involve sacral nutation and counternutation, altering pelvic dimensions.

content: Each sacroiliac joint is reinforced by a capsule and a strong network of ligaments. Anteriorly, there are two fasciae (not shown). Inferiorly, the sacrospinous and sacrotuberous ligaments connect the sacrum to the ischial spine and ischial tuberosity respectively. These ligaments tend to oppose adduction of the pelvis. There are also a series of posterior sacroiliac ligaments connecting the ilium to the lateral sacral crest. These ligaments tend to oppose abduction of the pelvis.
summary: The sacroiliac joint is stabilized by ligaments that resist adduction and abduction of the pelvis.

content: The transverse processes are long and have distinct tubercles on the end for muscle attachment. The lumbar spine is the next structure above the sacrum. It is concave at the back. This is the 'loin' area between the pelvis and the ribcage. The lumbar vertebrae are large and become larger at the bottom of the lumbar spine.
summary: The lumbar spine is located above the sacrum and consists of large vertebrae.

content: The lumbar spine is the next structure above the sacrum. This is the 'loin' area between the pelvis and the ribcage. The lumbar vertebrae are large and become larger at the bottom of the lumbar spine. The bodies of the vertebrae are large and shaped like a lima bean. They are concave posteriorly. The discs are thick, about one-third the thickness of the vertebral bodies. This increases mobility.
summary: Description of the lumbar spine's location, vertebrae size, shape, and the role of thick discs in mobility.

content: The transverse processes are long and have distinct tubercles on the end for muscle attachment. The articular processes project above and below the vertebral body, with a narrow part in-between: the isthmus. The superior articular processes of the vertebrae have a concave cylindrical form. They point medially (and slightly backward). The inferior articular processes have a convex cylindrical form. They point laterally (and slightly forward). These bony projections articulate with the neighboring vertebrae and are stacked on top of each other. Their articular facets are vertical and somewhat sagittal.
summary: Anatomical features of lumbar vertebrae, including transverse processes, articular processes, and their articulation.

content: The top lumbar vertebrae are sagittal. They become more and more frontal towards the lower lumbar vertebrae. At the lumbosacral junction, they are completely frontal. To summarize the mobility of the lumbar vertebrae: good range of motion for flexion, extension, and sidebending, limited ROM for rotation.
summary: Orientation of lumbar vertebrae facets and resulting range of motion (flexion, extension, sidebending are good, rotation is limited).

content: The lumbosacral joint is located between the sacrum and the 5th lumbar vertebra. The sacral base is tilted forward to a variable degree. The body of L5 and the disc between L5 and S1 are slightly thicker anteriorly than posteriorly. The L4/L5 joint also has a thicker anterior portion. Due to the oblique orientation of the sacrum, there are two perpendicular forces resulting from the weight of the upper body at L5. One force acts along the axis of the sacrum. The other force tends to make L5 slide forward. The contact between the articular facets of S1 and the inferior articular processes of L5 opposes the forward sliding tendency of L5.
summary: Description of the lumbosacral joint, its anatomy, and the forces acting upon it.

content: The lumbosacral joint is concave posteriorly. The ligament from L4 is oriented posteroinferiorly. The ligament from L5 runs more anteroinferiorly. Iliolumbar ligaments connect the transverse processes of L5 and L4 to the iliac crest. The iliolumbar ligaments tend to oppose sidebending. The L4 ligament is stretched and the L5 ligament relaxed during flexion. The reverse occurs during extension.
summary: Ligaments of the lumbar spine, their orientation, and how they influence movement.

content: The thoracic spine consists of twelve vertebrae, the thoracic vertebrae. The thoracic spine articulates with the ribs. There are posterior facets on the bodies of the thoracic vertebrae for articulation with the heads of ribs. The thickness of the discs in the thoracic region is only about one-sixth that of the bodies. This limits the range of motion in the thoracic spine.
summary: Description of the thoracic spine, its articulation with ribs, and the limited range of motion due to disc thickness.

content: The thoracic spine consists of twelve vertebrae, the thoracic vertebrae. The bodies of the thoracic vertebrae are cylindrical. The thickness of the discs in the thoracic region is only about one-sixth that of the bodies. This limits the range of motion in the thoracic spine. The facets of the articular processes in the thoracic region are round and flat. The articular facets are oriented postero-superolaterally on the superior articular processes and anteroinfero-medially on the inferior processes. This arrangement permits flexion, extension, and sidebending. The articular facets are located approximately on the circumference of a circle whose center would be the center of the vertebral body. This facilitates rotation.
summary: The thoracic spine has 12 vertebrae with cylindrical bodies and limited disc thickness, allowing for flexion, extension, sidebending, and rotation.

content: The thoracic spine articulates with the ribs. There are posterior facets on the bodies of the thoracic vertebrae for articulation with the heads of ribs. T1 has a facet near the top and an inferior facet. T2 through T9 each have superior and inferior facets. T10 through T12 each have one facet. The laminae of the thoracic spine are flat, rectangular, and taller than they are wide. The laminae of the thoracic spine are stacked on and overlap each other like tiles on a roof. The transverse processes of the thoracic spine decrease in length from top to bottom. The transverse processes of T1 through T10 have anterior facets for articulation with the tubercles of ribs. The spinous processes of the thoracic spine are elongated, laterally compressed, and directed inferiorly (except for T1 and T12). The characteristics of the thoracic spine help prevent hyperextension.
summary: The thoracic spine's vertebrae articulate with ribs through facets, with a unique arrangement of laminae and spinous processes that limit hyperextension.

content: The vertebral attachment to the rib cage tends to limit mobility of the thoracic spine. T1 through T7 between the scapulae have corresponding ribs (the 'true ribs') that are connected directly to the sternum by short pieces of cartilage which allow little mobility. Ribs 8, 9, and 10 (the 'false' ribs) have longer costal cartilages which attach to the cartilage of rib 7 rather than directly to the sternum. The mobility of T8, T9, and T10 is correspondingly greater. Ribs 11 and 12 (the 'floating' ribs) have no anterior attachment at all, so the mobility of T11 and T12 is greatest.
summary: The thoracic spine's mobility is influenced by rib attachments, with true ribs offering less mobility than false ribs and floating ribs providing the most freedom.

content: The thoracic cage consists of the thoracic vertebrae at the back, and the ribs and sternum in the front. The sternum is a flat bone located at the front of the thorax. The sternum consists of three parts: the manubrium, the body, and the xiphoid process. The top part of the manubrium articulates with the clavicles. The lateral edge of the body of the sternum has seven notches, which articulate with the first seven costal cartilages. The xiphoid process articulates with the costal cartilage. The first rib is the smallest and flattened from top to bottom. The rib is curved in three different ways: from above, the edge of the rib has the shape of a bucket handle, from the front, it has the shape of an italic S, and when we 'straighten' part of a rib, we can see torsion along its long axis. The ribs are elongated, flattened, and twisted bones. Each rib consists of a posterior end with three parts: the head, the neck, and the tubercle; a body; and an anterior end which.
summary: The thoracic cage is formed by the thoracic vertebrae, ribs, and sternum, with each component having specific features and articulations.

content: Most ribs articulate with two thoracic vertebrae at three points: the two facets on the head of the rib contact the vertebral bodies, and the tubercle contacts the transverse process. Exceptions are ribs 1, 11, and 12, which only contact one vertebral body, and ribs 11 and 12, which do not rest on a transverse process. Each joint is stabilized by numerous small ligaments. Anteriorly, each rib is attached to the sternum via its costal cartilage, which increases the elasticity of the thoracic cage. The first seven ribs are short and attach directly to the sternum. These ribs are called true ribs.
summary: Ribs primarily articulate with two vertebrae, with exceptions for ribs 1, 11, and 12. Costal cartilages connect ribs to the sternum, enhancing thoracic cage flexibility.

content: Most ribs articulate with two thoracic vertebrae at three points: the two facets on the head of the rib contact the vertebral bodies, and the tubercle contacts the transverse process. Exceptions are ribs 1, 11, and 12, which only contact one vertebral body, and ribs 11 and 12, which do not rest on a transverse process.
summary: Rib articulation: Most ribs connect to two vertebrae, while ribs 1, 11, and 12 have unique articulations.

content: Each joint is stabilized by numerous small ligaments. Anteriorly, each rib is attached to the sternum via its costal cartilage, which increases the elasticity of the thoracic cage. The first seven ribs are short and attach directly to the sternum. These ribs are called true ribs. The three following cartilages, which are longer, all attach to the 7th rib. These are called false ribs, and have greater mobility. The two lowest ribs do not have a cartilage. These are called floating ribs.
summary: Rib structure and attachment: Ribs connect to the sternum via cartilage, with variations in attachment and mobility.

content: The movements of a rib can be compared to those of a bucket handle. As the rib moves up or down, the diameter of the thoracic cage is changed. Posteriorly, the rib pivots on an axis passing through the center of two joints. One joint, with two facets, articulates with the vertebral body. Another joint articulates with the transverse process of the vertebra. At different levels, the spatial relationship of these two joints changes because the shape of the vertebra changes.
summary: Rib movement: Ribs function like bucket handles, changing thoracic cage diameter through pivoting on two joints.

content: For the superior thoracic vertebrae, the axis is directed more laterally, and elevation of the rib increases the anterior diameter of the thoracic cage. By contrast, for the inferior thoracic vertebrae, the axis is directed more posteriorly, and elevation of the rib increases the lateral diameter of the thoracic cage. The anterior ends of the ribs easily accommodate these movements because of the flexibility of the costal cartilages, which varies depending on the level of the rib. However, this flexibility can decrease with age, thus reducing overall mobility.
summary: Rib movement variation: Axis direction and costal cartilage flexibility influence rib movement and thoracic cage expansion.

content: During costal inhalation, the ribs are elevated. Therefore, the diameter of the upper thoracic cage is increased in an anterior direction, while that of the lower cage is increased in a lateral direction. The reverse occurs during costal exhalation, when the ribs move down. The costal cartilages undergo some torsion (twisting on their own axis) on inhalation, then return to their normal shape on exhalation.
summary: Rib movement during breathing: Inhalation elevates ribs, expanding the thoracic cage, while exhalation lowers them.

content: Obviously, spinal movements also affect the orientation of the ribs. The ribs in front move closer together during thoracic flexion. In right sidebending, the ribs move closer together on the right side and farther apart on the left side. The ribs move farther apart during extension. In right rotation, the ribs move posteriorly on the right and anteriorly on the left.
summary: Rib movement with spinal motion: Spinal flexion, sidebending, and rotation influence rib positioning.

content: The junction between the thoracic and lumbar spinal regions has some interesting features. T12 resembles T11 in its upper half, but resembles L1 in its lower half, i.e., the spinous process is shortened (which facilitates extension), and the inferior articular facets are large and convex (which restricts rotation). At T12/L1, the junction between the thoracic and lumbar spinal regions, mobility of the spine is characterized by good flexion, extension, and sidebending, but very little rotation.
summary: Thoracic-lumbar junction: T12 has unique features, and the T12/L1 joint allows for flexion, extension, and sidebending but limited rotation.

content: Because T11 and T12 are both attached to floating ribs, and because of the shape of the spinous process and inferior articular processes on T11, there is good range of motion in all directions at the T11/T12 joint: flexion, extension, sidebending, and rotation. Starting from the bottom of the spine, the T11/T12 joint is the first important rotational joint. Hyper-rotation is possible at this location.
summary: T11/T12 joint:  Allows for full range of motion, including rotation, due to its unique structure and floating ribs.

content: The cervical spine forms the skeleton of the neck. The cervical spine is divided into two regions: the suboccipital cervical spine and the lower cervical spine. The suboccipital cervical spine consists of the vertebrae C1 (atlas) and C2 (axis). C1 (atlas) is located just below the skull. C1 and C2 have a different shape and function differently from the other vertebrae.
summary: Cervical spine anatomy: Divided into suboccipital and lower regions, with C1 and C2 having unique structures and functions.

content: The lower cervical spine consists of vertebrae C3 through C7. The bodies of cervical vertebrae are small. The discs are about one-third as thick as the bodies.
summary: Lower cervical spine: Composed of C3-C7 vertebrae with smaller bodies and thinner discs compared to other regions.

content: C1 (atlas) is located just below the skull. The atlas has no vertebral body. The atlas is essentially a bony ring reinforced by two strong lateral masses. The atlas has no spinous process. The transverse ligament of the atlas attaches to the inside of the lateral masses and divides the ring of the atlas into two parts. The large transverse processes project from the sides of the atlas. The transverse processes of the atlas contain the transverse foramina, through which the vertebral artery passes. The anterior part of the transverse processes of the atlas surrounds the dens of the axis. The posterior part of the transverse processes of the atlas contains the vertebral foramen, through which the spinal cord passes. The upper and lower surfaces of the lateral masses of the atlas have facets for articulation with the occipital condyle at the top and the axis at the bottom.
summary: Description of the atlas (C1) structure, including its unique features and articulations.

content: C1 and C2 have a different shape and function differently from the other vertebrae. The atlas is the first vertebra at the top of the spine. The suboccipital spine is the top part of the cervical spine. The suboccipital spine is the area where movements which are independent of the head are made, like nodding the head 'yes' and shaking it 'no'.
summary: Distinctive characteristics and functions of the atlas (C1) and axis (C2) compared to other cervical vertebrae.

content: The lower cervical spine consists of vertebrae C3 through C7. The bodies of cervical vertebrae are small. The discs are about one-third as thick as the bodies. The rectangular shape of the cervical vertebral bodies in cross section somewhat restricts sidebending. The superior surfaces of the cervical vertebral bodies project upward on the sides (uncinate processes). The inferior surfaces of the cervical vertebral bodies are shaped to articulate with the superior ones. The vertebral bodies are stacked laterally. The superior surfaces of the cervical vertebral bodies are slightly convex and tilted forward. The inferior surfaces of the cervical vertebral bodies are slightly concave and tilted backward.
summary: Description of the structure and arrangement of cervical vertebrae C3-C7.

content: The lengths of the spinous processes of the cervical vertebrae vary. The spinous processes in the middle cervical area are short, especially C4, which permits good extension of the cervical spine. The spinous processes of C6 and C7 are long and restrain extension.
summary: Variations in the length of cervical vertebrae spinous processes and their influence on cervical spine extension.

content: Each cervical transverse process has two roots: one from the side of the body and the other from the pedicle. The two roots of the cervical transverse process merge to form a medial opening called the transverse foramen and a small lateral groove. The vertebral artery passes through the transverse foramina of C1-C6. Spinal nerves pass through the lateral grooves of the cervical transverse processes.
summary: Structure and function of cervical transverse processes, including their role in blood vessel and nerve passage.

content: The superior articular facets of the cervical vertebrae face posterosuperiorly, and the inferior facets anteroinferiorly, at an angle of 45°. Sidebending of the cervical spine is always accompanied by a certain amount of rotation.
summary: Orientation of cervical vertebral facets and their influence on cervical spine movement.

content: On each side of the foramen magnum are the occipital condyles, oval, convex surfaces covered with cartilage. The corresponding superior articular facets of the atlas are concave. The articulating surfaces of the occipital condyles and the atlas lie essentially on the outside of an imaginary sphere whose center is inside the skull. The occipital-atlas joint could be viewed as a ball-and-socket joint, potentially allowing movement in any direction. The shape of the joint surfaces (longer front-to-back than side-to-side) and presence of ligaments favor flexion/extension and restrict other movements.
summary: Description of the occipital-atlas joint, its structure, and range of motion.

content: There are four ligaments (two lateral, one anterior, one posterior) linking the atlas to the occipital bone. The same ligaments continue inferiorly onto the axis and help stabilize the axis-atlas joint.
