summary: Ligaments connecting the atlas to the occipital bone and their role in stabilizing the joint.

content: The articulating surfaces of the occipital condyles and the atlas lie essentially on the outside of an imaginary sphere whose center is inside the skull. The occipital-atlas joint could be viewed as a ball-and-socket joint, potentially allowing movement in any direction. However, the shape of the joint surfaces (longer front-to-back than side-to-side) and presence of ligaments favor flexion/extension and restrict other movements.
summary: The occipital-atlas joint, while resembling a ball-and-socket, primarily allows flexion/extension due to its shape and ligament structure.

content: There are four ligaments (two lateral, one anterior, one posterior) linking the atlas to the occipital bone. The same ligaments continue inferiorly onto the axis and help stabilize the axis-atlas joint. The foramen magnum is a large hole through which the spinal cord passes to merge with the brain.
summary: Ligaments connect the atlas to the occipital bone and axis, stabilizing the joint and allowing the spinal cord to pass through the foramen magnum.

content: The axis is the second cervical vertebra. The axis has the shape of a typical cervical vertebra, except that on the top, there are certain bony particularities which help it to articulate with the atlas. Each side of the vertebral body of the axis has an oval convex surface, which articulates with a lateral facet of the atlas above. The vertebral body of the axis has a peg-like process: this is the dens (odontoid process). The dens fits anteriorly against the anterior arch of C1 and posteriorly against an articular surface of the transverse ligament of C1.
summary: The axis, the second cervical vertebra, has a unique structure with a dens that articulates with the atlas, allowing for rotation.

content: The pivot joint of C1-C2 consists of a ring-like structure covered by cartilage rotating around the dens. The atlas (C1) presses on the axis (C2) and turns around its pivot. Rotation is the most important movement at the C1-C2 level, as in shaking your head 'no'. Rotation of C1 on C2 is accompanied by some lateral gliding of C2 which helps preserve the integrity of the spinal canal. C1 tends to move slightly closer to C2 during rotation.
summary: The C1-C2 joint is a pivot joint allowing primarily rotation, with slight lateral gliding to protect the spinal canal.

content: There are anterior and posterior ligaments between C2 and C1, as well as ligaments connecting the occiput to the body and dens of C2. The articular facets of both C1 and C2 are convex.
summary: Ligaments stabilize the C1-C2 joint, and the convex articular facets contribute to its movement.

content: The back side of the trunk contains numerous muscles, which are arranged in several layers. The deepest muscles in the back of the trunk attach only to the vertebrae and consist of small bundles that pass from one vertebra to the next.
summary: The back muscles are arranged in layers, with the deepest muscles connecting vertebrae.

content: The intertransverse muscles connect one transverse process to the next, posterior to the intertransverse ligament. The action of the intertransverse muscles is sidebending.
summary: The intertransverse muscles connect transverse processes and are responsible for sidebending.

content: The interspinalis muscles connect adjacent spinous processes on either side of the ligament. The action of the interspinalis muscles is extension. The interspinalis muscles are innervated by posterior branches of spinal nerves (C3-S4).
summary: The interspinalis muscles connect spinous processes and extend the spine, innervated by spinal nerves.

content: The transversospinalis muscles attach to the back of the vertebrae all along the spine, from sacrum to axis. The transversospinalis muscles consist of three bundles: the rotatores, the multifidus, and the semispinalis muscles. The rotatores pass toward the lamina of the vertebra above. The multifidus pass to the spinous processes of the vertebrae that are located two to four levels above. The semispinalis muscles pass to the spinous processes of the vertebrae that are located four to six levels above, covering the other layers.
summary: The transversospinalis muscles, spanning the entire spine, consist of three bundles (rotatores, multifidus, and semispinalis) with varying attachment points.

content: When viewed from behind, the transversospinalis form a 'chevron'-like pattern along the posterior spinal column. The muscle fibers of the transversospinalis run diagonally from inferior to superior when contracting bilaterally, moving the vertebrae into extension. The muscle fibers of the transversospinalis run diagonally from medial to lateral, causing sidebending. The muscle fibers of the transversospinalis run diagonally from anterior to posterior, causing rotation of the spine opposite the side of contraction.
summary: The transversospinalis muscles form a chevron pattern and contribute to extension, sidebending, and rotation of the spine.

content: The transversospinalis muscles are innervated by posterior branches of spinal nerves (C3-S4). Muscles located in the convex sagittal curves of the spine elicit a chain reaction. Electromyographic studies have shown that the activity and importance of the transversospinalis muscles are variable depending upon spinal level.
summary: The transversospinalis muscles are innervated by spinal nerves and their activity varies depending on the spinal level.

content: The activity of the transversospinalis muscles is most important around T6, where the posterior convexity of the thoracic spine is most pronounced. The activity of the transversospinalis muscles is less significant at T12. The activity of the transversospinalis muscles is also less significant around L3, where the posterior concavity of the lumbar region is most pronounced.
summary: The activity of the transversospinalis muscles is highest in the thoracic spine's convexity and decreases in the lumbar region's concavity.

content: Muscles located in the convex sagittal curves of the spine elicit a chain reaction. The activity of the transversospinalis muscles is most important around T6, where the posterior convexity of the thoracic spine is most pronounced. The activity of the transversospinalis muscles is less significant at T12. The activity of the transversospinalis muscles is also less significant around L3, where the posterior concavity of the lumbar region is most pronounced. The actions of the transversospinalis muscles are more predominant where the spine is most convex in the back.
summary: The transversospinalis muscles play a key role in spinal stability, with activity peaking in areas of convex curvature.

content: The longus colli muscle is located at the cervical level and helps maintain stability and alignment of the vertebrae and intervertebral discs. The psoas muscle is located at the lumbar level and helps maintain stability and alignment of the vertebrae and intervertebral discs.
summary: The longus colli and psoas muscles contribute to spinal stability and alignment.

content: The obliquus capitis superior originates from the transverse process of C1 and inserts on the occiput lateral to rectus capitis posterior major, just posterior to the mastoid process of the temporal bone. The obliquus capitis inferior runs from the spinous process of C2 to the transverse process of C1. The obliquus capitis superior, obliquus capitis inferior, and rectus capitis posterior major help produce extension at the C1/C2 joint when they contract bilaterally. Deep neck muscles are analogous to the transversospinalis but insert on the occiput.
summary: The obliquus capitis muscles and rectus capitis posterior major contribute to head extension and rotation.

content: The rectus capitis posterior minor runs from the posterior arch of C1 to the inferior occipital ridge. The rectus capitis posterior major originates from the spinous process of C2 and inserts just lateral to the minor. The three muscles that insert on the occiput bend the head to that side by contracting unilaterally. Obliquus capitis superior, with the most lateral insertion, does this most effectively. Obliquus capitis superior can produce left rotation by contracting on the right. These deep muscles have limited lever action because of their small size, but allow great precision of movement. In cooperation with the anterior neck muscles, they regulate the correct orientation of the head on the neck.
summary: The rectus capitis posterior muscles and obliquus capitis superior control head movement with precision.

content: The group of posterior muscles shown on this page forms a layer superficial to those described on the previous pages. These muscles are sometimes referred to collectively as the sacrospinalis or erector spinae. There are three muscles in this group: iliocostalis (most lateral), longissimus, and spinalis (most medial; see p. 80). Each of these is further divided into three subcomponents.
summary: The sacrospinalis or erector spinae muscles are a group of three muscles (iliocostalis, longissimus, and spinalis) that extend the spine.

content: Iliocostalis cervicis runs from the upper six ribs to the transverse processes of the lower cervicals. Iliocostalis thoracis runs from the lower six to the upper six ribs. Iliocostalis lumborum originates from the iliac crest via the lumbar fascia (a sheet-like structure made of dense connective tissue), and inserts on the lower ribs. Longissimus capitis originates from the transverse processes of T3 to C4 and inserts on the mastoid process of the temporal bone. Longissimus cervicis runs from the transverse processes of the upper thoracic vertebrae to the lower cervical vertebrae. Longissimus thoracis originates from the lumbar transverse processes and inserts on the thoracic transverse processes and posterior aspect of ribs 9 and 10. It fills the groove formed where the thoracic vertebrae meet the ribs.
summary: The iliocostalis and longissimus muscles extend the spine and contribute to head movement.

content: The main action of all these muscles is extension of the spine. Longissimus capitis contributes to extension of the head (bilateral contraction), or sidebending of the head (unilateral contraction). By contracting unilaterally, they also contribute to sidebending (especially iliocostalis lumborum, because of its position) and rotation. Innervation: posterior branches of spinal nerves (C2-L5)
summary: The iliocostalis, longissimus, and spinalis muscles primarily extend the spine, with additional roles in head and neck movement.

content: Spinalis capitis and semispinalis capitis originate from the spinous processes of C7—T1 and the transverse processes of C4—T4, respectively, and insert on the occiput. If spinalis capitis and semispinalis capitis contract bilaterally while the cervical spine is fixed, they extend the head. If spinalis capitis and semispinalis capitis contract unilaterally with the spine fixed, they can contribute to sidebending or rotation. If spinalis capitis and semispinalis capitis contract bilaterally with the head fixed, they tend to straighten out the cervical spine. Spinalis capitis and semispinalis capitis are innervated by posterior branches of spinal nerves (C1-C5).
summary: Spinalis capitis and semispinalis capitis muscles contribute to head extension, sidebending, and rotation.

content: Spinalis capitis and semispinalis capitis originate from the spinous processes of C7—T1 and the transverse processes of C4—T4, respectively, and insert on the occiput. If spinalis capitis and semispinalis capitis contract bilaterally while the cervical spine is fixed, they extend the head. If spinalis capitis and semispinalis capitis contract unilaterally with the spine fixed, they can contribute to sidebending or rotation. If spinalis capitis and semispinalis capitis contract bilaterally with the head fixed, they tend to straighten out the cervical spine. Spinalis capitis and semispinalis capitis are innervated by posterior branches of spinal nerves (C1-C5).
summary: Spinalis capitis and semispinalis capitis are muscles that extend, rotate, and sidebend the head and neck.

content: Spinalis thoracis runs from the spinous processes of T1 through T10 to the spinous processes of T11 through L2. Spinalis thoracis extends the spine in the thoracic region. Spinalis thoracis is innervated by posterior branches of spinal nerves (C2—T10).
summary: Spinalis thoracis is a muscle that extends the thoracic spine.

content: The back muscles described thus far (and some later) form a deep muscle layer, also referred to as the spinal muscles. The spinal muscles have a short lever arm, hence not much power to do certain activities, e.g., extension of the spine from the horizontal position. The spinal muscles work very precisely. In the vertical position, the spinal muscles work together to keep the spine straight, rebalancing each other slightly at every vertebral level. In a standing person, the spinal muscles are almost constantly working. The spinal muscles are very active muscles, able to work without fatiguing for long stretches of time. The head 'sits on the neck' for an entire day with the help of the spinal muscles.
summary: The spinal muscles are a deep layer of muscles that maintain spinal stability and posture.

content: Splenius capitis originates from the nuchal ligament and the spinous processes of C7 through T3-T4 and inserts on the mastoid process and adjacent occipital bone. Splenius capitis is innervated by posterior branches of spinal nerves (C1-C8). Splenius cervicis runs from the spinous process of T5-T7 to the transverse processes of C1-C3. Contracting bilaterally, splenius cervicis extends the head and cervical spine. Contracting unilaterally, splenius cervicis causes sidebending and rotation toward the contracting side.
summary: Splenius capitis and cervicis extend, rotate, and sidebend the head and neck.

content: Levator scapulae runs from the transverse processes of C1-C4 to the medial scapula. Levator scapulae primarily acts on the scapula; however, when the scapula is fixed, its actions can reinforce those of the splenius cervicis.
summary: Levator scapulae primarily acts on the scapula but can also assist in head and neck movements.

content: Serratus posterior superior runs from the spinous processes of C7 to T3 and inserts on the first five ribs. Serratus posterior superior elevates the ribs and thereby aids in inspiration. Serratus posterior superior is innervated by branches of the first four intercostal nerves (T1-T4).
summary: Serratus posterior superior elevates the ribs and assists in inspiration.

content: Serratus posterior inferior runs from the spinous processes of T12 to L2 and inserts on the last four ribs. Serratus posterior inferior depresses these ribs and thereby aids in expiration. Serratus posterior inferior is innervated by superior branches of the last four intercostal nerves.
summary: Serratus posterior inferior depresses the ribs and assists in expiration.

content: Rhomboids, when the scapula is fixed, pull the vertebrae laterally.
summary: Rhomboids pull the vertebrae laterally when the scapula is fixed.

content: Latissimus dorsi, when acting bilaterally, extends the thoracolumbar spine. Latissimus dorsi, by wrapping around the posteroinferior trunk, helps preserve the structural integrity of the trunk during certain movements.
summary: Latissimus dorsi extends the spine and supports trunk stability.

content: The trapezius extends the neck when the shoulder bones are fixed and bilaterally contracted. Unilateral contraction of the superior portion of the trapezius can assist in ipsilateral sidebending or contralateral rotation of the head.
summary: Trapezius extends the neck and assists in head sidebending and rotation.

content: Longus colli is a deep muscle consisting of three portions. The longitudinal portion of longus colli runs from the bodies of C2 through T3 to the bodies of C4-C7. The oblique superior portion of longus colli runs from the anterior arch of C1 to the transverse processes of C3-C6. The oblique inferior portion of longus colli runs from the bodies of T1-T3 to the transverse processes of C5-C7. Bilateral contraction of longus colli flexes the head and straightens the cervical spine. Unilaterally, longus colli assists in sidebending and flexion of the head. Longus colli is innervated by the cervical plexus (C2-C8).
summary: Longus colli is a deep neck muscle with three portions that flexes, rotates, and sidebends the head.

content: The oblique superior portion of longus colli runs from the anterior arch of C1 to the transverse processes of C3-C6. The oblique inferior portion of longus colli runs from the bodies of T1-T3 to the transverse processes of C5-C7. Bilateral contraction of longus colli flexes the head and straightens the cervical spine. Unilaterally, longus colli assists in sidebending and flexion of the head. Longus colli is innervated by the cervical plexus (C2-C8).
summary: Longus colli, innervated by the cervical plexus, has two portions and functions in head flexion, cervical spine stabilization, and sidebending.

content: Rectus capitis lateralis runs from the jugular process of the occipital bone to the transverse process of the atlas. Bilateral contraction of rectus capitis lateralis flexes the head relative to the atlas. Unilateral contraction of rectus capitis lateralis assists in sidebending. Rectus capitis anterior runs from the occipital bone to the anterior atlas. Bilateral contraction of rectus capitis anterior flexes the head relative to the atlas. Unilateral contraction of rectus capitis anterior assists in sidebending and rotation on the side of the contraction.
summary: Rectus capitis lateralis and anterior muscles originate from the occipital bone and atlas, contributing to head flexion and sidebending.

content: Longus capitis originates from the transverse processes of C3-C6 and inserts on the occiput just anterior to the rectus. Longus colli and longus capitis work together with the scalenes to stabilize the cervical spine, which becomes fixed during inspiration. The muscles that attach to the cervical spine and the occiput help straighten the upper cervical spine and flex the head (bilateral contraction) or sidebend the head (unilateral contraction). These muscles are innervated by the cervical plexus (C1-C4).
summary: Longus capitis, along with longus colli and scalenes, stabilizes the cervical spine, particularly during inspiration, and contributes to head flexion and sidebending.

content: The scalenes consist of three muscles: Scalenus anterior, Scalenus medius, and Scalenus posterior. Scalenus anterior and Scalenus medius originate from the transverse processes of C3-C6 and C2-C7 respectively. Scalenus anterior and Scalenus medius insert close together on the anterior part of rib 1. Scalenus posterior runs from the transverse processes of C4-C6 to the lateral surface of rib 2. The scalenes are innervated by the brachial plexus (C4-C8). The orientation of Scalenus posterior is almost vertical, whereas Scalenus anterior runs obliquely forward.
summary: The scalenes (anterior, medius, and posterior) originate from cervical vertebrae and insert on ribs 1 and 2, contributing to respiration and cervical spine movement.

content: When the ribs are fixed, unilateral contraction of the scalenes (especially posterior) produces sidebending of the cervical spine. Medius and anterior also produce some contralateral rotation. Bilateral contraction of medius and anterior accentuates the curvature of the spine. When the cervical (and upper thoracic) spine is fixed, contraction of the scalenes elevates ribs 1 and 2, assisting in inspiration. Longus colli and the scalenes play an important role in stabilizing the cervical spine during this action.
summary: Scalenes, particularly the posterior, contribute to cervical spine sidebending and rotation, and assist in respiration by elevating ribs 1 and 2.

content: Suprahyoid muscles include hyoglossus, geniohyoid, mylohyoid, digastric, and stylohyoid. Infrahyoid muscles include sternohyoid, thyrohyoid, and omohyoid. Among other actions, these muscles assist with the flexion of the head above the neck and thorax.
summary: Suprahyoid and infrahyoid muscles, located in the neck, contribute to head and neck movement, including flexion.

content: Sternocleidomastoid has dual origins on the sternum and clavicle, near their junction on the manubrium. Sternocleidomastoid inserts on the mastoid process and the curved superior occipital line. Sternocleidomastoid (SCM) is the largest and most important anterior neck muscle. SCM is innervated by the spinal accessory (11th cranial nerve) and cervical plexus (C1-C2).
summary: Sternocleidomastoid, the largest anterior neck muscle, originates on the sternum and clavicle and inserts on the mastoid process and occipital bone.

content: When the skull is fixed, SCM elevates the sternum and clavicle, and thereby assists in inspiration. When the thoracic cage is fixed, unilateral contraction of SCM causes ipsilateral sidebending and contralateral rotation of the head, as well as extension. Bilateral contraction results in extension of the head, accentuating cervical lordosis.
summary: Sternocleidomastoid assists in both respiration and head movement, contributing to sidebending, rotation, and extension.

content: The intercostal muscles occupy the spaces between adjacent ribs and are arranged in two thin layers. The fibers of the internal intercostals run downward and backward to the upper border of the rib below. The external intercostals run downward and forward to the rib below. The intercostals form a muscular sheet, which joins the ribs to each other, making the thoracic cage a contiguous entity.
summary: Intercostal muscles, arranged in two layers, connect ribs and contribute to the structural integrity and movement of the thoracic cage.

content: The intercostal muscles occupy the spaces between adjacent ribs and are arranged in two thin layers. The fibers of the internal intercostals run downward and backward to the upper border of the rib below. The external intercostals run downward and forward to the rib below. The intercostals form a muscular sheet, which joins the ribs to each other, making the thoracic cage a contiguous entity.
summary: The intercostal muscles, arranged in two layers, connect the ribs and form a continuous thoracic cage.

content: The levatores costarum run from the transverse process of a thoracic vertebra to the tubercle of a rib located one or two levels below. The levatores costarum assist in rotation of the spine or elevation of the ribs, depending on which end is fixed.
summary: The levatores costarum muscles connect vertebrae to ribs, aiding in spine rotation and rib elevation.

content: The transversus thoracis originates from the posterior surface of the lower sternum and xiphoid process and runs superolaterally to insert on the cartilages of ribs 2 through 6. The contraction of the transversus thoracis lowers these ribs, assisting in expiration.
summary: The transversus thoracis muscle originates from the sternum and inserts on ribs 2-6, assisting in expiration by depressing the ribs.

content: The diaphragm is a big flat muscle with 'radiating' fibers located inside the thoracic cage. The diaphragm extends like a dome between the thoracic and abdominal cavities. The diaphragm inserts on its own central tendon, which is composed of strong interlacing fibrous tissue and shaped somewhat like a three-leafed clover. The diaphragm is pierced by three large openings for the inferior vena cava, esophagus, and descending aorta in the middle and near the vertebral column. The sternal origin of the diaphragm is from the xiphoid process. The costal origin of the diaphragm is from the deep surfaces of ribs 7-12 and their cartilages; the fibers of attachment interdigitate with those of the transversus abdominis muscle. The vertebral origin of the diaphragm consists of a 'right crus' arising directly from the bodies of L1-L3, a 'left crus' arising from the bodies of L1-L2, and five arcuate ligaments. The single median arcuate ligament joins the two crura at the midline and arches over the abdominal entrance of the aorta. The paired medial arcuate ligaments extend from the body of L1 to its transverse processes, arching over the psoas major muscle. The paired lateral arcuate ligaments extend from the transverse processes of L1 to rib 12, arching over the quadrates lumborum muscle.
summary: The diaphragm is a dome-shaped muscle separating the thoracic and abdominal cavities, playing a crucial role in respiration.

content: The diaphragm is the principal muscle of inspiration. The diaphragm is innervated by the phrenic nerves (C3-C5).
summary: The diaphragm is the primary muscle responsible for inhalation, innervated by the phrenic nerves.

content: The psoas major originates from the sides of the lumbar vertebrae. The psoas major inserts on the hip. The psoas major is a multi-articular muscle that passes over eight joints, six of which are intervertebral. The psoas major can straighten the spine in combination with the posterior transversospinalis muscles. Bilateral contraction of the psoas major and posterior transversospinalis muscles can erect the lumbar spine. Unilateral contraction of the psoas major pulls the lumbar spine into sidebending, flexion, and rotation of the side opposite the contraction. The psoas major is innervated by the lumbar plexus (L1-L3).
summary: The psoas major is a multi-articular muscle originating from the lumbar vertebrae and inserting on the hip, playing a role in spinal movement and posture.

content: The quadratus lumborum originates from the posterior iliac crest and inserts on rib 12 and the transverse processes of L1-L5. The quadratus lumborum is composed of both vertical and oblique fibers. Contraction of the quadratus lumborum pulls on rib 12 and the other ribs, causing sidebending of the lumbar spine and ribcage. The quadratus lumborum is an expiratory muscle. When the ribs and spine are fixed, the quadratus lumborum raises the pelvis on one side. The quadratus lumborum is innervated by the lumbar plexus (T12/L1-L3).
summary: The quadratus lumborum muscle, originating from the iliac crest and inserting on ribs and vertebrae, contributes to sidebending, respiration, and pelvic movement.

content: There are four paired muscles in the abdominal wall. The transversus abdominis is the deepest of the four abdominal muscles. The transversus abdominis attaches below to the inguinal ligament and the iliac crest, posteriorly to the five lumbar vertebrae, above to the inner surfaces of the last seven ribs, and anteriorly to the linea alba. Contraction of the transversus abdominis reduces the diameter of the abdomen. When the vertebrae are fixed, the transversus abdominis pulls in the belly.
summary: The transversus abdominis, the deepest abdominal muscle, plays a crucial role in compressing the abdomen and stabilizing the core.

content: There are four paired muscles in the abdominal wall. The transversus abdominis is the deepest of the four abdominal muscles. The transversus abdominis attaches below to the inguinal ligament and the iliac crest, posteriorly to the five lumbar vertebrae, above to the inner surfaces of the last seven ribs, and anteriorly to the linea alba. Contraction of the transversus abdominis reduces the diameter of the abdomen. When the vertebrae are fixed, the transversus abdominis pulls in the belly. When the anterior aponeurosis of the transversus abdominis is fixed, it increases lordosis of the lumbar spine. The transversus abdominis is innervated by the intercostal nerves (T7-T12), ilioinguinal and iliohypogastric nerves (L1).
summary: Description of the transversus abdominis muscle, its attachments, actions, and innervation.

content: The internal oblique muscle lies between the transversus and external oblique muscles. The internal oblique muscle is attached below to the inguinal ligament and the iliac crest. The internal oblique muscle is attached posteriorly to the lumbodorsal fascia. The internal oblique muscle is attached above to the lower four ribs. The internal oblique muscle is attached anteriorly to a very broad aponeurosis. The fibers of the internal oblique muscle run in various directions depending on their location. The average direction of the internal oblique muscle fibers is anterosuperior. Unilateral contraction of the internal oblique muscle results in sidebending or ipsilateral rotation of the spine and ribcage. If the pelvis is fixed, unilateral contraction of the internal oblique muscle acts on the ribs. If the ribs are fixed, unilateral contraction of the internal oblique muscle acts on the pelvis. Bilateral contraction of the internal oblique muscle causes compression of the abdomen and assists in flexion of the trunk when the pelvis is fixed. When the vertebrae and pelvis are fixed, bilateral contraction of the internal oblique muscle lowers the ribs and moves them backward. The internal oblique muscle is involved in expiration. The internal oblique muscle is innervated by the intercostal nerves (T9-T12), ilioinguinal and iliohypogastric nerves (L1).
summary: Description of the internal oblique muscle, its attachments, actions, and innervation.

content: The external oblique muscle is attached above to the outer surfaces of ribs 5-12. The fibers of the external oblique muscle intertwine with those of the serratus anterior. The external oblique muscle is attached to the ilioinguinal ligament. The external oblique muscle forms a broad aponeurosis in front and below, ending at and contributing to the linea alba and inguinal ligament. The average direction of the external oblique muscle fibers is anteroinferior. Unilateral contraction of the external oblique muscle results in side-bending and contralateral rotation of the spine and ribcage. Bilateral contraction of the external oblique muscle causes flexion of the trunk. The oblique muscles work synergistically in rotation of the trunk. Rotation of the trunk to the left involves simultaneous contraction of the left internal oblique and right external oblique. The external oblique muscle is innervated by the intercostal nerves (T5-T12) and ilioinguinal and iliohypogastric nerves (L1). When the pelvis is fixed, the external oblique muscle lowers the ribs and is then an expiratory muscle.
summary: Description of the external oblique muscle, its attachments, actions, and innervation.

content: The rectus abdominis muscle is located anteriorly inside a 'rectus sheath' formed by the aponeuroses of the three preceding muscles. The rectus abdominis muscle runs from the crest and symphysis of the pubis to the xiphoid process and cartilages of ribs 5-7. The rectus abdominis muscle adheres to the anterior layer of its sheath at three tendinous intersections. The tendinous intersections are visible as transverse grooves when the muscle contracts. The rectus abdominis muscle is the most direct flexor of the trunk by moving the sternum toward the pelvis. The rectus abdominis muscle can also flex the trunk by tilting the pelvis backward. The rectus abdominis muscle is innervated by the last four intercostal nerves (T5-T12) and ilioinguinal and iliohypogastric nerves (L1).
summary: Description of the rectus abdominis muscle, its attachments, actions, and innervation.

content: Levator ani originates from fascia covering the obturator internus, along a line extending from the posterior body of the pubis to the ischial spine.
summary: Description of the origin of the levator ani muscle.

content: The tendinous intersections are visible as transverse grooves when the muscle contracts. The rectus abdominis muscle is the most direct flexor of the trunk by moving the sternum toward the pelvis. The rectus abdominis muscle can also flex the trunk by tilting the pelvis backward. The rectus abdominis muscle is innervated by the last four intercostal nerves (T5-T12) and ilioinguinal and iliohypogastric nerves (L1).
summary: The rectus abdominis muscle is the primary flexor of the trunk, innervated by multiple nerves, and exhibits visible tendinous intersections.

content: Levator ani originates from fascia covering the obturator internus, along a line extending from the posterior body of the pubis to the ischial spine. The fibers from the left and right sides of the levator ani muscle run inferomedially and meet each other at the midline. Some posterior fibers of the levator ani muscle insert on the coccyx and lower sacrum. Levator ani contains an opening for the anal canal. The anterior portion of the levator ani muscle is different in the female pelvis, where it has an opening to accommodate the vulva. In the male pelvis, the anterior portion of the levator ani muscle is closed. Innervation of the levator ani muscle is the accessory branch of the sacral plexus (S3).
summary: The levator ani muscle, with distinct anatomical features and innervation, supports pelvic organs and contains openings for the anal canal and vulva.

content: Coccygeus is posterior to levator ani, running from the ischial spine to the coccyx and lower sacrum. Both levator ani and coccygeus muscles support the weight of the pelvic organs and are involved in continence. Levator ani and coccygeus muscles rotate the sacrum backward. Innervation of the coccygeus muscle is the accessory branch of the pudendal plexus (S4). Levator ani and coccygeus muscles do not play a role in the positioning of the pelvis on top of the legs since they do not insert into the femurs.
summary: The coccygeus muscle, working alongside the levator ani, supports pelvic organs, aids in continence, and rotates the sacrum.

content: The pelvic diaphragm forms the floor of the pelvis and consists of two paired muscles: levator ani and coccygeus. Inferior to the pelvic diaphragm and overlapping it anteriorly is the urogenital diaphragm.
summary: The pelvic diaphragm, composed of the levator ani and coccygeus muscles, forms the floor of the pelvis, with the urogenital diaphragm situated below.

content: The abdominal cavity is inferior to the thorax and contains the abdominal organs. The abdominal cavity is bounded above by the diaphragm, lower ribs, costal cartilages, and sternum. The abdominal cavity is bounded posteriorly by the lumbar vertebrae. The abdominal cavity is bounded laterally and anteriorly by the abdominal muscles. The abdominal cavity is bounded inferiorly by the pelvis and the pelvic and urogenital diaphragm. The abdomen can be compared to a liquid-filled, flexible container which can change its shape but not its volume (i.e., is non-compressible).
summary: The abdominal cavity, containing abdominal organs, is anatomically defined and functions as a non-compressible container.

content: The thorax can be compared to a gas-filled container which can change its shape and is compressible. The diaphragm is like a plunger that moves between the thorax and abdomen and helps control the shape, volume, and pressure of both cavities during activities such as breathing, speaking, coughing, defecating, childbirth, and hiccups.
summary: The thorax, a compressible container, and the diaphragm, a crucial muscle for breathing and other functions, interact to regulate thoracic and abdominal volumes and pressures.

content: During normal inspiration, contraction of the diaphragm lowers its dome and increases the volume of the thorax and lungs. The increase in volume during inspiration results in a decrease in pressure inside the lungs, which causes air to enter from the outside environment. Contraction of the abdominal muscles can cause the abdomen to maintain its shape and resist lowering of the diaphragm.
summary: Diaphragmatic contraction during inspiration increases lung volume and decreases pressure, allowing air intake. Abdominal muscle contraction can resist diaphragm movement.

content: In the case of abdominal muscle contraction, the central tendon becomes the fixed point and contraction of the fibers of the diaphragm results in elevation of the ribs. The elevation of the ribs during contraction of the diaphragm is due to the superomedial orientation of the fibers and lateral pressure from the contents of the abdomen, which are being compressed from above. During normal expiration, the diaphragm simply relaxes (i.e., the dome moves upward) and the elastic lung tissue returns to its normal size after being stretched during inspiration. The decrease in lung volume during expiration results in an increase in pressure, and air is expelled through the nose or mouth.
summary: Diaphragm contraction during forced expiration elevates the ribs, while relaxation during normal expiration allows lung volume to decrease and air to be expelled.

content: The lungs do not empty completely during expiration. In 'forced' (or active) expiration, the expiratory muscles, especially the abdominal muscles, contract and press the abdominal organs upward against the thorax and lower ribcage, further decreasing lung volume and increasing the pressure which drives air out. No matter how hard 'forced' expiration is done, some air will always remain in the lungs. This is called residual volume.
summary: Even during forced expiration, residual volume remains in the lungs, preventing complete emptying.

content: The shoulder is the area where the arm is attached to the thorax. The shoulder involves more than one joint in contrast to the hip. The shoulder has two important functions: flexibility to allow the hand and arm a large range of motion and providing a strong, stable fixed point for actions like lifting heavy objects or pushing against resistance. The primary joint of the shoulder is the glenohumeral joint between the head of the humerus and the glenoid cavity of the scapula.
summary: The shoulder, a complex structure with multiple joints, provides both flexibility and stability for arm movement.

content: The shoulder is the area where the arm is attached to the thorax. The shoulder involves more than one joint in contrast to the hip. The shoulder has two important functions: flexibility to allow the hand and arm a large range of motion and providing a strong, stable fixed point for actions like lifting heavy objects or pushing against resistance. The primary joint of the shoulder is the glenohumeral joint between the head of the humerus and the glenoid cavity of the scapula. The scapula is an extremely mobile bone and is connected to the axial skeleton (sternum) only by the long, thin clavicle.
summary: The shoulder is a complex joint structure responsible for both flexibility and stability.

content: The shoulder comprises three joints plus important gliding planes. We can distinguish functional areas of the shoulder: the scapulothoracic and the scapulohumeral. There are two types of shoulder movements: movements of the shoulder on the thorax and combined actions of the scapula and arm.
summary: The shoulder functions through a combination of three joints and distinct movement patterns.

content: Shoulder movements on the thorax include elevation, depression, abduction, adduction, lateral rotation, and medial rotation. Elevation is the movement of the shoulder upward. Depression is the movement of the shoulder downward. Abduction is the movement of the shoulder away from the spine. Adduction is the movement of the shoulder closer to the spine. Lateral rotation is the tilt of the shoulder by moving the inferior angle away from the midline. Medial rotation is the tilt of the shoulder by moving the inferior angle of the scapula toward the midline.
summary: Shoulder movements on the thorax involve both upward/downward and inward/outward motions.

content: The combined actions of the scapula and arm allow the arm to move anteriorly (flexion), posteriorly (extension), laterally (abduction), and medially (adduction). Anteriorly (flexion) is the movement of the arm beyond 90 degrees. Posteriorly (extension) is the movement of the arm, with a smaller range than flexion. Laterally (abduction) is the movement of the arm beyond 90 degrees, closer to the midline. Medially (adduction) is the movement of the arm closer to the body. Adduction is combined with extension to move the arm behind the body or with flexion to move the arm in front of the body.
summary: Combined scapula and arm movements allow for a wide range of arm positions.

content: Rotation of the humerus (on its axis) is best visualized with the elbow bent to avoid confusion with pronation and supination of the forearm. Medial rotation is the rotation of the humerus inward. Lateral rotation is the rotation of the humerus outward.
summary: The humerus can rotate inward and outward, independent of other arm movements.

content: At their extremes, arm movements affect the thoracic spine and ribcage. Flexion of the arms is associated with extension of the spine and 'opening' of the anterior ribcage (i.e., ribs move apart from each other). Extension of the arms causes flexion of the spine and 'closing' of the ribcage. Adduction is associated with ipsilateral sidebending and closing of the ipsilateral hemithorax. Abduction causes contralateral sidebending and opening of the ipsilateral hemithorax. Medial or lateral rotation of the humerus is associated with similar rotation of the spine.
summary: Shoulder movements have a reciprocal relationship with the spine and ribcage.

content: The shoulder girdle consists of the scapulae (posteriorly) and the clavicles and sternum (anteriorly). The clavicle (collarbone) is a flattened, elongated bone with two bends in it, so that it is roughly S-shaped when viewed from above or below. The clavicle articulates medially with the manubrium of the sternum. Laterally, the clavicle articulates with the acromion of the scapula. The medial end of the clavicle is roughly triangular with a rounded articular surface which is concave transversely and convex vertically (saddle-shaped). The medial end of the clavicle meets an inversely-shaped articular surface on the manubrium. The clavicle can move upward (elevation), rotate around its longitudinal axis, move backward (extension), move forward (flexion), and move downward (depression). These movements generally occur secondarily in association with movements of the scapula.
summary: The shoulder girdle is composed of the scapulae, clavicles, and sternum, with the clavicle playing a key role in connecting the scapula to the axial skeleton.

content: No matter how hard 'forced' expiration is done, some air will always remain in the lungs. This is called residual volume.
summary: Residual volume is the amount of air remaining in the lungs after maximal exhalation.

content: The clavicle can move upward (elevation), rotate around its longitudinal axis, move backward (extension), move forward (flexion), and move downward (depression). These movements generally occur secondarily in association with movements of the scapula.
summary: The clavicle's movements are primarily associated with scapular motion.

content: The medial end of the clavicle is roughly triangular with a rounded articular surface which is concave transversely and convex vertically (saddle-shaped). The medial end of the clavicle meets an inversely-shaped articular surface on the manubrium.
summary: The clavicle's medial end articulates with the manubrium, forming a saddle-shaped joint.

content: Laterally, the clavicle articulates with the acromion of the scapula. The shape of the articular surfaces allows some gliding movement, as well as opening and closing of the angle formed by the two bones.
summary: The clavicle articulates with the scapula at the acromioclavicular joint, allowing for gliding and angular movement.

content: The capsule of the acromioclavicular joint is loose and the joint is supported by four ligaments: the superior and inferior acromioclavicular ligaments, which inhibit the opening of the angle between the two bones; and the conoid and trapezoid ligaments, which inhibit the closing of the angle. These two ligaments are stretched between the coracoid process and the clavicle.
summary: The acromioclavicular joint is stabilized by ligaments that restrict its opening and closing.

content: The scapula, or shoulderblade, is a flat triangular bone with two surfaces (anterior and posterior), three borders, and three angles. The lateral border of the scapula is much thicker than the medial border. The lateral angle of the scapula contains the glenoid cavity, a shallow oval-shaped depression which articulates with the head of the humerus.
summary: The scapula is a triangular bone with a glenoid cavity that articulates with the humerus.

content: The anterior surface of the scapula is smooth and slightly concave and leans in a mobile fashion against the ribcage. The posterior surface of the scapula is convex.
summary: The scapula has a concave anterior surface and a convex posterior surface.

content: The spine of the scapula is a strong, sharp ridge running diagonally near the superior border. There are depressions above and below the spine called the supraspinous and infraspinous fossae. The scapular spine is a triangular ridge, which starts roughly perpendicular to the scapular plane. The posterior side of the spine is thick, tilting in two directions. The inferior portion of the spine has an enlarged tubercle for attachment of the trapezius.
summary: The scapula's spine runs diagonally across its superior border, with depressions above and below it.

content: The lateral end of the spine is enlarged and flattened to form the acromion process. Note that the acromion is oriented perpendicular to the spine. The posterior surface of the acromion process can easily be palpated through the skin. The anterior surface of the acromion process projects beyond the glenoid cavity; its anterior side consists of an oval articular surface, which articulates with the lateral edge of the clavicle.
summary: The acromion process is a flattened extension of the scapula's spine, articulating with the clavicle.

content: The mobility of the sternoclavicular and acromioclavicular joints allows the scapula to move in various directions. In elevation, the scapula moves upward and away from the ribcage. In depression, the scapula moves downward and fits more snugly against the ribcage. In retraction (adduction), the medial border of the scapula moves closer to the vertebral column. In protraction (abduction), the medial border of the scapula moves away from the vertebral column, and the scapula moves anteriorly at a 45° angle as it glides on the convex thorax.
summary: The scapula's mobility is facilitated by its articulation with the clavicle and sternum, allowing for elevation, depression, retraction, and protraction.

content: To understand rotation, visualize a mobile scapula positioned above the ribcage and moving around an axis, which is vertical to the ribcage and passes under the middle of the scapular spine. The scapula pivots around this axis. The glenoid cavity of the scapula can point in many directions. This greatly increases the range of motion of the glenohumeral joint. Mobility of the arm is greatly increased by such movements of the scapula.
summary: The scapula can rotate around an axis, increasing the range of motion of the glenohumeral joint and arm mobility.

content: The free movement of the scapula is aided by the presence of some fatty layers ('gliding planes'). One is located between the serratus anterior muscle and the ribcage. Another is located between the subscapularis and the serratus anterior muscles.
summary: Fatty layers between muscles facilitate the scapula's free movement.

content: This is the long bone of the upper arm. Landmarks of the proximal end of the humerus include the lateral greater tubercle, the anterior lesser tubercle for muscle attachment, and the bicipital (intertubercular) groove running between the two tubercles.
summary: The humerus is the long bone of the upper arm, with notable landmarks at its proximal end.

content: The free movement of the scapula is aided by the presence of some fatty layers ('gliding planes'). One is located between the serratus anterior muscle and the ribcage. Another is located between the subscapularis and the serratus anterior muscles.
summary: The scapula's movement is facilitated by fatty gliding planes between the serratus anterior and ribcage, and between the subscapularis and serratus anterior.

content: The humerus is the long bone of the upper arm. Landmarks of the proximal end of the humerus include the lateral greater tubercle, the anterior lesser tubercle for muscle attachment, and the bicipital (intertubercular) groove running between the two tubercles. The shaft itself, in cross section, is circular near the proximal end but becomes more triangular (with a pronounced anterior ridge) near the distal end.
summary: The humerus, the upper arm bone, has proximal landmarks like tubercles and a groove, and its shape transitions from circular to triangular distally.

content: The triangular shape of the humerus here allows us to distinguish three surfaces and three ridges. The medial head whose spheroid surface articulates with the glenoid cavity of the scapula. The anatomical neck is just below the head. The distal end is enlarged and forms articulating surfaces for the bones of the forearm at the elbow joint.
summary: The humerus's distal end features three surfaces and ridges, including the medial head that articulates with the scapula and the anatomical neck.

content: The glenohumeral joint unites the head of the humerus with the glenoid fossa of the scapula. The articular surfaces of the glenohumeral joint consist of the head of the humerus and the glenoid cavity of the scapula. The head of the humerus is oriented mostly medial, but also slightly posterosuperior. The surface area of the head of the humerus is two or three times larger than that of the glenoid cavity. The glenoid cavity is fairly shallow.
summary: The glenohumeral joint connects the humerus head to the scapula's glenoid fossa, with the humerus head being larger and the glenoid cavity shallow.

content: The glenoid cavity is oval and slightly concave anteriorly. The glenoid cavity is oriented mostly lateral, but also slightly anterosuperior. The glenoid labrum is a fibrocartilaginous ring that attaches to the glenoid cavity and increases the fit of the shoulder joint surfaces.
summary: The glenoid cavity is oval, slightly concave, and oriented laterally, with the glenoid labrum enhancing joint fit.

content: The capsule of the glenohumeral joint attaches to the scapula on the outer rim of the glenoid cavity. The capsule of the glenohumeral joint attaches anterosuperiorly to the coracoid process. The capsule of the glenohumeral joint encircles the origin of the long head of the biceps. The capsule of the glenohumeral joint attaches around the head of the humerus. The capsule of the glenohumeral joint has many folds, especially in its inferior portion. The capsule of the glenohumeral joint gives it a good range of motion for anterior flexion and abduction.
summary: The glenohumeral joint capsule attaches to the scapula, coracoid process, humerus, and biceps origin, providing a wide range of motion.

content: The capsule of the glenohumeral joint is reinforced at the top and front by ligaments. The coracohumeral ligament runs from the coracoid process and forms two bundles that run to the greater and lesser tubercle of the humerus. The coracohumeral ligament is the strongest ligament of this joint. Ligaments run from the border of the glenoid cavity to the anatomical neck of the humerus. The ligaments consist of three bundles: superior, middle, and inferior.
summary: The glenohumeral joint capsule is reinforced by ligaments, including the strong coracohumeral ligament and three bundles connecting the glenoid cavity to the humerus.

content: There are weak areas between the ligaments. The capsuloligamentary structure of the shoulder is not very strong. The glenohumeral part of the shoulder is stabilized especially by the deepest muscles, which form a cuff of 'active ligaments' around the shoulder, called the rotator cuff.
summary: While ligaments provide support, the shoulder's capsuloligamentary structure is relatively weak, relying heavily on the rotator cuff muscles for stability.

content: The coracoacromial ligament stretches across the scapula and attaches to the acromion and the coracoid process. The coracoacromial ligament protects the tendon of the supraspinatus. If the humerus is lifted too much, the coracoacromial ligament can rub against the tendon and can paradoxically cause it to wear out.
summary: The coracoacromial ligament protects the supraspinatus tendon but can cause wear if the humerus is excessively lifted.

content: The resting position of the joint is where the arm is in slight flexion, abduction, and internal rotation.
summary: The shoulder joint's resting position involves slight flexion, abduction, and internal rotation.

content: The serratus anterior muscle is a broad, thin muscle covering the lateral ribcage. The serratus anterior muscle originates from the upper ten ribs and inserts along the entire medial border of the scapula. When the ribs are fixed, the serratus anterior muscle flattens the medial border of the scapula against the ribcage. With the upper fibers, the serratus anterior muscle pulls the scapula laterally (abduction) and into upward rotation.
summary: The serratus anterior muscle, originating from the ribs and inserting on the scapula, flattens the scapula against the ribcage and contributes to its abduction and upward rotation.

content: The resting position of the joint is where the arm is in slight flexion, abduction, and internal rotation. The fibers of the serratus anterior muscle are contracted and visible when the arm is pushing against some resistance. In actions such as push-ups, where great force is being exerted by the arms, the serratus anterior muscle keeps the scapula fixed in place and tight against the ribcage.  In situations where great force is being exerted by the arms, the middle fibers of trapezius (an adductor) and serratus (an abductor) contract simultaneously to stabilize the scapula.
summary: Describes the resting position of the arm and the role of the serratus anterior and trapezius muscles in stabilizing the scapula during forceful arm movements.

content: The serratus anterior muscle is a broad, thin muscle covering the lateral ribcage. The serratus anterior muscle originates from the upper ten ribs and inserts along the entire medial border of the scapula. When the ribs are fixed, the serratus anterior muscle flattens the medial border of the scapula against the ribcage. With the upper fibers, the serratus anterior muscle pulls the scapula laterally (abduction) and into upward rotation. The serratus anterior muscle is innervated by the long thoracic nerve (C5-C7). There are some fatty layers ('gliding planes') separating serratus from the ribcage and from the subscapularis muscle. These fatty layers increase the mobility of the scapula and are important in many complex movements of the shoulder.
summary: Provides a detailed anatomical description of the serratus anterior muscle, including its origin, insertion, actions, innervation, and its role in scapular mobility.

content: If the scapula is fixed, the lower fibers of serratus anterior lift the middle ribs, acting as inspiratory muscles. The subclavius muscle runs from rib 1 and its cartilage to the underside of the clavicle. The pectoralis minor originates from ribs 3-5 and inserts on the coracoid process. When the ribs are fixed, the pectoralis minor muscle pulls the scapula downward and forward, as if to tilt the scapula above the ribcage by lifting the inferior angle upward. When the scapula is fixed, the pectoralis minor muscle assists in inspiration by elevating the ribs. The pectoralis minor muscle is innervated by the medial pectoral nerve (C7-T1).
summary: Describes the actions of the serratus anterior and pectoralis minor muscles, including their roles in respiration and scapular movement.

content: The sternocleidomastoid (SCM) acts primarily on the head and cervical spine. If the head is fixed, the SCM can elevate the area where the clavicle and sternum meet, and thereby assist in inspiration. The action of the sternocleidomastoid is to depress the clavicle.
summary: Describes the primary actions of the sternocleidomastoid muscle, including its role in respiration and head movement.

content: The levator scapulae originates from the transverse processes of C1-C4 and inserts on the superior angle of the scapula. The actions of the levator scapulae are to elevate the scapula and rotate it downward; when the scapula is fixed, it acts on the cervical spine. The rhomboids (major and minor) originate from the spinous processes of C7 and T1-T4 and insert on the medial border of the scapula. The actions of the rhomboids are to adduct the scapula and rotate it downward; when the scapula is fixed, they act on the thoracic spine.
summary: Describes the origin, insertion, and actions of the levator scapulae and rhomboid muscles, which contribute to scapular movement and stability.

content: The levator scapulae, rhomboids, and upper trapezius elevate the scapula. The lower trapezius and lower serratus anterior depress the scapula. The serratus anterior abducts the scapula. The trapezius and rhomboids adduct the scapula. The serratus anterior, upper trapezius, and lower trapezius upward rotate the scapula. The rhomboids and levator scapulae downward rotate the scapula.
summary: Lists the primary actions of various muscles involved in scapular movement, including elevation, depression, abduction, adduction, upward rotation, and downward rotation.

content: The orientation of trapezius fibers ranges from inferolateral to superolateral. Simultaneous contraction of all trapezius fibers adducts the scapula. The upper fibers of the trapezius act in elevation and upward rotation of the scapula. The lower fibers of the trapezius act in depression and upward rotation of the scapula. The trapezius originates on the occiput, nuchal ligament, and spinous processes of the cervical vertebrae and thoracic vertebrae down to T12. The trapezius inserts on the lateral third of the clavicle and acromion (upper fibers), scapular spine (middle fibers), and a tubercle at the medial end of the scapular spine (lower fibers).
summary: Describes the anatomical features and actions of the trapezius muscle, including its origin, insertion, fiber orientation, and contributions to scapular movement.

content: The subscapularis originates from the anterior surface of the scapula and inserts on the lesser tubercle of the humerus. The subscapularis fibers converge at the lateral angle of the scapula. The subscapularis is the principal muscle of medial rotation of the arm. The subscapularis is innervated by the upper subscapular nerve (C5-C6).
summary: Provides a detailed anatomical description of the subscapularis muscle, including its origin, insertion, actions, and innervation.

content: The trapezius inserts on the lateral third of the clavicle and acromion (upper fibers), scapular spine (middle fibers), and a tubercle at the medial end of the scapular spine (lower fibers).
summary: Description of the trapezius muscle's insertion points.

content: The subscapularis originates from the anterior surface of the scapula and inserts on the lesser tubercle of the humerus. The subscapularis fibers converge at the lateral angle of the scapula. The subscapularis is the principal muscle of medial rotation of the arm. The subscapularis is innervated by the upper subscapular nerve (C5-C6).
summary: Description of the subscapularis muscle's origin, insertion, function, and innervation.

content: The supraspinatus originates from the supraspinous fossa on the posterior scapula. The supraspinatus tendon passes under the acromioclavicular joint and the ligament connecting the coracoid process to the acromion. The supraspinatus inserts on the highest point on the greater tubercle. A large bursa surrounds the supraspinatus tendon, separating it from the inferior surface of the acromion and deltoid muscle. The bursa surrounding the supraspinatus tendon acts as an auxiliary component of the glenohumeral joint. Adhesions around the supraspinatus bursa can restrict shoulder mobility. The supraspinatus abducts the arm; its action is weak but coupled with that of the deltoid. The supraspinatus is innervated by the subscapular nerve (C5-C6).
summary: Description of the supraspinatus muscle's origin, insertion, tendon path, surrounding bursa, function, and innervation.

content: The infraspinatus originates from the infraspinous fossa. The infraspinatus tendon passes above the capsule of the glenohumeral joint and inserts on the greater tubercle. The infraspinatus insertion point is posteroinferior to the insertion of the supraspinatus. The infraspinatus actions are lateral rotation and participation in abduction. The infraspinatus is innervated by the suprascapular nerve (C4-C6).
summary: Description of the infraspinatus muscle's origin, insertion, function, and innervation.

content: The teres minor originates from the lateral border of the scapula (posterior surface) and inserts on the greater tubercle below the insertion of the infraspinatus. The teres minor action is lateral rotation. The teres minor is innervated by the axillary nerve (C5-C6).
summary: Description of the teres minor muscle's origin, insertion, function, and innervation.

content: The rotator cuff muscles are composed of the subscapularis, supraspinatus, infraspinatus, and teres minor. The rotator cuff muscles tendons surround and reinforce the shoulder-joint capsule on three sides. The rotator cuff muscles mobilize the humerus and act as 'active ligaments' providing mobility to the joint. The supraspinatus prevents the humerus from being dislocated/displaced upward. The subscapularis prevents the head of the humerus from gliding backward, forward, and backward. The infraspinatus and teres minor prevent the humerus from being dislocated forward.
summary: Description of the rotator cuff muscles, their composition, function, and role in shoulder stability.

content: The shoulder is a joint whose bony structure and passive attachments (capsule, ligaments) are only weak stabilizing structures. The main stabilizing force of the shoulder comes from the action of the periarticular muscles. The action of the periarticular muscles can be upset, which is why the shoulder is often a problem area causing joint pain.
summary: Explanation of the shoulder joint's stability, emphasizing the role of periarticular muscles.

content: The coracobrachialis muscle arises from the coracoid process and inserts on the medial surface of the humeral shaft, near the middle. The action of the coracobrachialis muscle is to flex and adduct the arm. The coracobrachialis muscle is innervated by the musculocutaneous nerve (C6-C7).
summary: Description of the coracobrachialis muscle's origin, insertion, function, and innervation.

content: The biceps brachii muscle participates in flexion of the shoulder. The long head of the biceps brachii muscle is involved in abduction of the arm. The short head of the biceps brachii muscle is involved in adduction of the arm. The triceps brachii muscle participates in adduction of the arm.
summary: Description of the biceps brachii and triceps brachii muscles' actions on the shoulder and arm.

content: The pectoralis major muscle has a clavicular head that originates from the anterior, medial clavicle and a sternocostal head that originates from the sternum and costal cartilages 1-6 and rib 7. The tendon of the pectoralis major muscle is twisted such that the fibers from the clavicular head insert below those from the sternocostal head on the lateral aspect of the bicipital groove. When the ribcage is fixed, all the fibers of the pectoralis major muscle adduct and medially rotate the arm.
summary: Description of the pectoralis major muscle's heads, tendon insertion, and action.

content: The short head of the biceps brachii muscle is involved in adduction of the arm. The triceps brachii muscle participates in adduction of the arm.
summary: The biceps brachii and triceps brachii muscles both contribute to arm adduction.

content: The pectoralis major muscle has a clavicular head that originates from the anterior, medial clavicle and a sternocostal head that originates from the sternum and costal cartilages 1-6 and rib 7. The tendon of the pectoralis major muscle is twisted such that the fibers from the clavicular head insert below those from the sternocostal head on the lateral aspect of the bicipital groove.
summary: The pectoralis major muscle has two heads with distinct origins, and its tendon inserts in a specific way.

content: When the ribcage is fixed, all the fibers of the pectoralis major muscle adduct and medially rotate the arm. The superior fibers of the pectoralis major muscle are involved in flexion up to 60°, then the inferior fibers take over and continue flexion up to 0°.
summary: The pectoralis major muscle's actions on the arm include adduction, medial rotation, and flexion.

content: When the shoulder is fixed, the superior fibers of the pectoralis major muscle lower the clavicle and the inferior fibers participate in inspiration. When the shoulder is fixed while the arm is flexed, all the fibers of the pectoralis major muscle are involved in inspiration stretching of pectoralis major.
summary: The pectoralis major muscle has additional roles in clavicle movement and respiration.

content: The pectoralis major muscle is innervated by the lateral and medial pectoral nerves (C5-T1).
summary: The pectoralis major muscle is innervated by the lateral and medial pectoral nerves.

content: The latissimus dorsi muscle originates from the sacral and iliac crests, thoracolumbar fascia, spinous processes of T7-T12, and posterior surfaces of the four lower ribs. The tendon of the latissimus dorsi muscle wraps around the medial side of the humerus, makes a twist, and inserts on the bicipital groove.
summary: The latissimus dorsi muscle has a wide origin and inserts on the bicipital groove.

content: The actions of the latissimus dorsi muscle are extension, adduction, and medial rotation of the arm. If the humerus is fixed, the actions of the latissimus dorsi muscle are as described on page 82.
summary: The latissimus dorsi muscle primarily extends, adducts, and medially rotates the arm.

content: The latissimus dorsi muscle is innervated by the thoracodorsal nerve (C6-C8).
summary: The latissimus dorsi muscle is innervated by the thoracodorsal nerve.

content: The teres major muscle originates from the posterior surface of the inferior angle of the scapula and inserts on the medial aspect of the bicipital groove, next to the latissimus dorsi muscle. The actions of the teres major muscle are the same as those of the latissimus dorsi muscle, but much less powerful.
summary: The teres major muscle shares similar actions with the latissimus dorsi muscle but with less force.

content: The deltoid muscle is superficial and gives the shoulder its characteristic shape. The deltoid muscle consists of three groups of fibers: middle, posterior, and anterior.
summary: The deltoid muscle is a superficial muscle that shapes the shoulder and has three fiber groups.

content: The middle fibers of the deltoid muscle attach to the lateral border of the acromion. The posterior fibers of the deltoid muscle attach to the spine of the scapula (inferior portion of the posterior border). The anterior fibers of the deltoid muscle attach to the clavicles (lateral third of the anterior scapular border). The three groups of deltoid fibers converge toward the middle of the arm and insert on the lateral surface of the humerus.
summary: The deltoid muscle has three distinct fiber groups that attach to different bony landmarks and converge to insert on the humerus.

content: Contraction of the anterior deltoid muscle fibers causes flexion and medial rotation of the arm. Contraction of the middle deltoid muscle fibers causes abduction of the arm. Contraction of the posterior deltoid muscle fibers causes extension of the arm.
summary: The deltoid muscle's three fiber groups produce flexion, abduction, and extension of the arm.

content: The deltoid muscle is innervated by the axillary nerve (C5-C6).
summary: The deltoid muscle is innervated by the axillary nerve.

content: Muscle actions in shoulder flexion include: anterior deltoid, pectoralis major, and coracobrachialis. Accessory muscles include biceps brachii and subscapularis.
summary: The primary muscles for shoulder flexion are the anterior deltoid, pectoralis major, and coracobrachialis, with accessory muscles like the biceps brachii and subscapularis.

content: Muscle actions in shoulder extension include: posterior deltoid, latissimus dorsi, and teres major. Accessory muscles include teres minor, short head of biceps, long head of triceps, and coracobrachialis.
summary: The primary muscles for shoulder extension are the posterior deltoid, latissimus dorsi, and teres major, with accessory muscles like the teres minor, short head of biceps, long head of triceps, and coracobrachialis.

content: Muscle actions in shoulder abduction include: deltoid and supraspinatus. Accessory muscles include infraspinatus and long head of biceps.
summary: The primary muscles for shoulder abduction are the deltoid and supraspinatus, with accessory muscles like the infraspinatus and long head of biceps.

content: Muscle actions in shoulder lateral rotation include: infraspinatus, teres minor, and posterior deltoid.
summary: The primary muscles for shoulder lateral rotation are the infraspinatus, teres minor, and posterior deltoid.

content: Muscle actions in shoulder adduction include: latissimus dorsi, pectoralis major, and teres major.
summary: The primary muscles for shoulder adduction are the latissimus dorsi, pectoralis major, and teres major.

content: The subscapularis, latissimus dorsi, pectoralis major, teres major, and anterior deltoid muscles are involved in medial rotation of the shoulder.
summary: The subscapularis, latissimus dorsi, pectoralis major, teres major, and anterior deltoid muscles contribute to shoulder medial rotation.

content: Muscle actions in the shoulder change depending on the arm's position.
summary: Shoulder muscle actions are dependent on the arm's position.

content: Adductor and medial rotator muscles are predominant in shoulder muscle actions.
summary: Adductor and medial rotator muscles are the most important for shoulder actions.

content: The pectoralis major muscle is a flexor of the arm up to 60 degrees. Beyond 90 degrees, the pectoralis major muscle functions as an extensor, bringing the arm back toward anatomical position.
summary: The pectoralis major muscle acts as a flexor up to 60 degrees and an extensor beyond 90 degrees.

content: The elbow joint allows the upper limb to fold on itself and extend. The elbow joint enables flexion and extension actions, allowing the distance between the shoulder and hand to be shortened or lengthened. The elbow joint participates in rotating the forearm around its longitudinal axis. The elbow joint's pronation-supination action multiplies the possible positions of the hand.
summary: The elbow joint facilitates flexion, extension, rotation, and pronation-supination, allowing for a wide range of hand positions.

content: The elbow joint allows the upper limb to fold on itself and extend. The elbow joint enables flexion and extension actions, allowing the distance between the shoulder and hand to be shortened or lengthened. The elbow joint participates in rotating the forearm around its longitudinal axis. The elbow joint's pronation-supination action multiplies the possible positions of the hand.
summary: The elbow joint facilitates flexion, extension, rotation, and pronation-supination, enabling a wide range of arm movements.

content: Flexion of the elbow joint decreases the angle between the anterior surfaces of the arm and forearm. In active elbow flexion, the movement is limited by contact between the bodies of the muscles involved. In passive elbow flexion, the angle may become slightly smaller since the muscles are more compressible. Extension of the elbow is a return from flexion to anatomical position, which is an increase in the angle between the arm and forearm. Generally, an increase of the elbow angle past 180° is impossible due to contact between the olecranon process and its fossa. In a few exceptional individuals, the elbow angle can become slightly larger than 180°; this is called 'recurvation'.
summary: Elbow flexion decreases the angle between the arm and forearm, while extension increases it.  The range of motion is limited by anatomical structures and muscle contact.

content: Beyond 90 degrees, the pectoralis major muscle functions as an extensor, bringing the arm back toward anatomical position.
summary: The pectoralis major muscle acts as an extensor at angles beyond 90 degrees.

content: The forearm consists of two long bones: the radius and the ulna. Each of the radius and ulna has a shaft and two extremities. The radius and ulna are triangular shaped and therefore have three surfaces and three edges. The proximal part of the radius is small, while its distal part is large. The proximal extremity of the radius consists of two parts: the head, which is covered by cartilage, and the neck. The head of the radius consists of a top part (beveled medially) and a circumference. At its distal part, the medial edge of the radius bifurcates, so that the cross section of the bone is quadrangular. The inferior surface of the radius articulates with the wrist. The bifurcation of the radius has a concave articular surface which articulates with the ulna, which is the ulnar notch of the radius. There is another protuberance at the lateral distal end of the radius: the styloid process. The shaft of the radius has a cylindrical shape with three surfaces and three edges.
summary: The forearm is composed of the radius and ulna, each with a shaft and two extremities. The radius is characterized by its proximal-distal size difference and distal bifurcation, while the ulna has a larger proximal end with prominent processes.

content: The proximal part of the ulna is large, while its distal part is small. The proximal part of the ulna has two large processes: the olecranon and the coronoid process. The distal extremity of the ulna is called the ulnar head. Laterally, the ulnar head has a convex articular surface which articulates with the radius. Medially, the ulnar head has a protuberance, the styloid process. Attached to the inferior surface of the ulna is a triangular ligament, which connects the ulna to the bones of the wrist.
summary: The ulna is characterized by its large proximal end with olecranon and coronoid processes, and a smaller distal end (ulnar head) that articulates with the radius and connects to wrist bones.

content: The humerus has two lateral bony projections, the medial and lateral epicondyles. Posteriorly, there is an olecranon fossa (not shown). The two protuberances (medial and lateral epicondyles) delimit a triangular space. At the base of this triangle, there are two articular surfaces: the medial pulley-shaped surface is oblique medioinferiorly and articulates with the trochlear notch of the ulna. This is the trochlea of the humerus. The lateral rounded surface with a diameter of about 1cm is the capitulum.
summary: The humerus features epicondyles, a triangular space, and articular surfaces (trochlea and capitulum) that connect with the ulna and radius.

content: At the distal end of the humerus, the anterior edge bifurcates and the bone flattens out, becomes larger, and curves forward. Just above the trochlear eminence is the coronoid fossa anteriorly and the olecranon fossa posteriorly. Just above the capitulum is the radial fossa of the humerus.
summary: The distal humerus exhibits specific features like a bifurcated anterior edge, a flattened and curved shape, and distinct fossae (coronoid, olecranon, and radial).

content: The surfaces between the shafts of the radius and ulna are connected via a thin layer: the interosseous membrane. At the head of the radius, on its upper surface, is a shallow cup or fovea which articulates with the capitulum. The radius is lined by a beveled layer, which articulates with the interosseous membrane.
summary: The radius and ulna are connected by the interosseous membrane. The radius features a fovea that articulates with the capitulum, and its beveled layer connects with the interosseous membrane.

content: At the base of this triangle, there are two articular surfaces: the medial pulley-shaped surface is oblique medioinferiorly and articulates with the trochlear notch of the ulna. This is the trochlea of the humerus. The lateral rounded surface with a diameter of about 1cm is the capitulum. The surfaces between the shafts of the radius and ulna are connected via a thin layer: the interosseous membrane. At the head of the radius, on its upper surface, is a shallow cup or fovea which articulates with the capitulum. The radius is lined by a beveled layer, which articulates with the interosseous membrane.
summary: Describes the anatomical features of the humerus and radius, focusing on their articular surfaces and connections.

content: The anterior surface of the olecranon and the superior surface of the coronoid process form an almost continuous surface in the shape of a hollow cylinder. This surface is the trochlear notch of the ulna. The trochlear notch of the ulna is covered with cartilage and separated into two grooves by a longitudinal ridge. The trochlear notch articulates with the trochlea of the humerus.
summary: Details the structure and articulation of the trochlear notch of the ulna with the humerus.

content: From an anatomical point of view, the radial notch of the ulna is not part of the elbow joint because it is not contained in the same capsule. The olecranon consists of five surfaces, including a beak-like projection on its superior surface. The coronoid process has four surfaces, with a beak-like projection of its own on its anterior surface.
summary: Explains the anatomical distinction of the radial notch and describes the olecranon and coronoid process of the ulna.

content: Three bones—the humerus, ulna, and radius—come together to form the capsule of the elbow joint. The capsule attaches to the circumference of the coronoid and olecranon fossae of the humerus and connects with the medial and lateral epicondyles without encircling them. The capsule attaches to the circumference of the neck and the radius. The capsule also attaches to the circumference of the trochlear notch of the ulna. The capsule is taut in front (especially laterally), but loose in back (facilitating flexion).
summary: Describes the structure and attachments of the elbow joint capsule.

content: The anterior and posterior elbow ligaments are minor ligaments. Posteriorly, the fibers of the anterior and posterior elbow ligaments cross over. Anteriorly, they are fan-shaped and reinforce the capsule.
