content: The posterior group of leg muscles is the most important muscle group. The deeper layer of the posterior group of leg muscles consists of three muscles located on the posterior surfaces of the tibia and fibula. The Flexor digitorum longus originates from the posteromedial tibial shaft, runs posterior to the medial malleolus and sustentaculum tali, along the plantar surface of the foot, and inserts on distal phalanges II-V. Tibialis posterior is the deepest calf muscle. Tibialis posterior arises from the posterosuperior tibial and fibular shafts and interosseous membrane, and passes posterior to the medial malleolus and anterior to sustentaculum tali. The primary insertion of tibialis posterior is on a prominent medial tubercle of navicular, but it also inserts on the cuboid, lateral cuneiform, and metatarsals II-IV. Actions of tibialis posterior: plantar flexion of toes II-V and ankle, inversion of foot, support of arches. Tibialis posterior is the most powerful flexor of toes II-V. Tibialis posterior also assists in plantar flexion, supination, and adduction of the foot, the latter counteracted by quadratus plantae.
summary: The posterior group of leg muscles, particularly the tibialis posterior, flexor digitorum longus, and other deep calf muscles, are crucial for plantar flexion, inversion, and support of the arches of the foot.

content: Tibialis posterior arises from the posterosuperior tibial and fibular shafts and interosseous membrane, and passes posterior to the medial malleolus and anterior to sustentaculum tali. The primary insertion of tibialis posterior is on a prominent medial tubercle of navicular, but it also inserts on the cuboid, lateral cuneiform, and metatarsals II-IV. Actions of tibialis posterior: plantar flexion of toes II-V and ankle, inversion of foot, support of arches. Tibialis posterior is the most powerful flexor of toes II-V. Tibialis posterior also assists in plantar flexion, supination, and adduction of the foot, the latter counteracted by quadratus plantae. Innervation of tibialis posterior: tibial nerve (S1-S3). The role of tibialis posterior, in conjunction with peroneus longus, in forming a 'sling' for the middle foot, was noted on the preceding page.
summary: Description of the tibialis posterior muscle: origin, insertion, actions, innervation, and role in foot support.

content: Flexor hallucis longus arises from the posteroinferior fibula and interosseous membrane, runs posterior to the medial malleolus, along a groove on the posterior talus, behind sustentaculum tali, along the medial plantar surface of the foot, and inserts on distal phalanx I. Flexor hallucis longus is important in the propulsion phase of walking. Actions of flexor hallucis longus: plantar flexion of big toe and ankle, inversion, support of medial arch; assists in plantar flexion and adduction of foot. Flexor hallucis longus has a very important role in stabilizing the foot when standing on tiptoe, since the pushing action of the big toe offsets the anterior loss of balance. Flexor hallucis longus also helps stabilize the ankle. Innervation of flexor hallucis longus: tibial nerve (S1-S3).
summary: Description of the flexor hallucis longus muscle: origin, insertion, actions, innervation, and role in walking and balance.

content: The superficial group of posterior calf muscles is known collectively as the triceps surae. Triceps surae is the strongest muscle group of the leg. Triceps surae consists of three muscular heads, all of which terminate on the Achilles tendon, which then attaches to the posterior surface of the calcaneus. Soleus is the deepest head of triceps surae. Soleus arises from the posterosuperior tibia and fibula. Soleus crosses two joints: the ankle and the subtalar joint. Innervation of soleus: common tibial nerve (L5/S2). Actions of triceps surae: Together, the three muscles pull the calcaneus into plantar flexion under the talus, with a tendency to inversion. Soleus is covered by the two superficial heads of gastrocnemius, which originate at the distal posterior femur from a tendon attaching to the back of each condyle. Gastrocnemius shapes the outline of the posterior calf. Gastrocnemius crosses three articulations: the ankle, subtalar joint, and knee. Innervation of gastrocnemius: common tibial nerve (S1-S2). Gastrocnemius indirectly pulls the talus into plantar flexion. This second action is actually more important than the first because it gives the joint more mobility. The gastrocnemius muscle crosses the knee.
summary: Description of the triceps surae muscle group, including its composition, actions, and innervation.

content: Plantar flexion of the ankle by the triceps surae is necessary but not sufficient for standing on tiptoe. Dorsiflexion at the ankle stretches the soleus muscle. When the knee is very flexed, the gastrocnemius is slack and inefficient as an ankle flexor. When the knee is extended or slightly flexed, the gastrocnemius is more taut and efficient as an ankle flexor. To stretch the gastrocnemius, knee extension is required. When the knee is flexed and the leg and foot bear the body's weight, the gastrocnemius and hamstrings combine their forces to become knee extensors. When the foot is not bearing the body's weight, the gastrocnemius and hamstrings act as knee flexors.
summary: The role of the gastrocnemius and soleus in ankle and knee movement, including their function in standing on tiptoe and stretching.

content: The tibialis anterior, extensor hallucis longus, and extensor digitorum longus tendons, passing anterior to the bimalleolar axis, perform dorsiflexion. The peroneus tertius tendon also performs dorsiflexion. The peroneus longus, peroneus brevis, triceps surae, flexor hallucis longus, and tibialis posterior tendons, passing posterior to the bimalleolar axis, perform plantar flexion. The extensor hallucis longus, tibialis anterior, tibialis posterior, flexor digitorum longus, and flexor hallucis longus tendons, passing medial to the longitudinal axis of the foot, perform inversion/adduction.
summary: The actions of various tendons in the foot, categorized by their location and function (dorsiflexion, plantar flexion, inversion/adduction).

content: The squat is a compound exercise that primarily targets the quadriceps, glutes, and hamstrings. Research by Schoenfeld (2010) showed that deep squats performed below parallel produced 7% greater glute activation than parallel squats. A typical squat prescription for strength development is 3-5 sets of 5-8 reps at 70-85% of 1RM.
summary: The squat, a compound exercise targeting the quadriceps, glutes, and hamstrings, is more effective for glute activation when performed below parallel.

content: The tibialis anterior, extensor hallucis longus, and extensor digitorum longus tendons, passing anterior to the bimalleolar axis, perform dorsiflexion. The peroneus tertius tendon also performs dorsiflexion.
summary: Dorsiflexion of the foot is primarily performed by the tibialis anterior, extensor hallucis longus, extensor digitorum longus, and peroneus tertius tendons.

content: The peroneus longus, peroneus brevis, triceps surae, flexor hallucis longus, and tibialis posterior tendons, passing posterior to the bimalleolar axis, perform plantar flexion. The triceps surae also contributes to inversion.
summary: Plantar flexion of the foot is primarily performed by the peroneus longus, peroneus brevis, triceps surae, flexor hallucis longus, and tibialis posterior tendons.

content: The extensor hallucis longus, tibialis anterior, tibialis posterior, flexor digitorum longus, and flexor hallucis longus tendons, passing medial to the longitudinal axis of the foot, perform inversion/adduction.
summary: Inversion/adduction of the foot is primarily performed by the extensor hallucis longus, tibialis anterior, tibialis posterior, flexor digitorum longus, and flexor hallucis longus tendons.

content: The peroneus longus and brevis, peroneus tertius, and extensor digitorum longus (lateral part) tendons, passing lateral to the longitudinal axis of the foot, perform eversion/abduction.
summary: Eversion/abduction of the foot is primarily performed by the peroneus longus and brevis, peroneus tertius, and extensor digitorum longus (lateral part) tendons.

content: Plantar flexion is dominant over dorsiflexion, and inversion/adduction is dominant over eversion/abduction.
summary: The foot exhibits a natural dominance in plantar flexion and inversion/adduction compared to dorsiflexion and eversion/abduction.

content: The talus fits more snugly into the 'pincer' formed by the distal tibia and fibula during dorsiflexion than plantar flexion. The peroneus longus, peroneus brevis, flexor hallucis longus, and tibialis posterior muscles pull down the fibula to compensate for the loss of stability during plantar flexion. The fit of the 'pincer' on the plantar-flexed talus is improved when the lateral malleolus is lowered.
summary: The ankle's stability is influenced by the fit of the talus within the 'pincer' formed by the tibia and fibula, with dorsiflexion providing a tighter fit and plantar flexion requiring muscular compensation.

content: The flexor hallucis longus and tibialis posterior muscles tighten the two sides of the 'pincer'. When the fibula is lowered, the distal tibiofibular ligaments come under tension, tightening the 'pincer'. These effects on the 'pincer' by muscles and ligaments stabilize the ankle during plantar flexion.
summary: Muscles and ligaments work together to stabilize the ankle during plantar flexion by tightening the 'pincer' formed by the tibia and fibula.

content: The foot has three arches. The arches of the foot rest on three points of support. The arches of the foot constitute a flexible slat that serves as a shock absorber and adapts the foot's shape to the ground.
summary: The foot's three arches provide shock absorption and adaptability to the ground, resting on three points of support.

content: When standing, the weight of the body is distributed among three points. The posteroinferior tuberosity of the calcaneus receives most of the weight. The head of metatarsal I is the secondary weight-bearing point. The head of metatarsal V supports the least weight.
summary: Weight distribution in a standing position primarily rests on the calcaneus, with secondary support from metatarsal I and minimal support from metatarsal V.

content: The medial arch is formed primarily by five bones (calcaneus, talus, navicular, medial cuneiform, metatarsal I), four ligaments (talocalcaneal, calcaneonavicular, and small ligaments joining the cuneiform to navicular and metatarsal), and four muscles (abductor hallucis, tibialis posterior, peroneus longus, flexor hallucis longus). Flexor hallucis longus has three functions with respect to the medial arch: 1. stretches the arch like the string of a bow 2. supports the calcaneus by passing under the sustentaculum tali 3. supports the talus by passing along its posterior groove.
summary: The medial arch is a complex structure formed by bones, ligaments, and muscles, with the flexor hallucis longus playing a crucial role in its support and function.

content: The lateral arch is not as high as the medial one. The lateral arch is formed primarily by three bones (calcaneus, cuboid, metatarsal V), three ligaments (short plantar [calcaneocuboid] ligament, long plantar ligament, plantar aponeurosis), and two muscles (peroneus brevis, peroneus longus). Peroneus longus has two functions here: supports the calcaneus by passing under the peroneal tubercle supports the cuboid.
summary: The lateral arch, while less prominent than the medial arch, is still crucial for foot stability and is formed by bones, ligaments, and the peroneus longus muscle.

content: The transverse arch is most visible around the middle of the metatarsals. The transverse arch is higher on the medial (navicular) side than the lateral (cuboid) side. The transverse arch's muscular support comes primarily from adductor hallucis (transverse head), peroneus longus, tibialis posterior, and the interossei.
summary: The transverse arch, located across the metatarsals, is higher on the medial side and receives support from various muscles.

content: Cervical spine refers to the top seven vertebrae of the spine. The atlas is the first cervical vertebra. The axis is the second cervical vertebra. Cervical vertebrae are the bones that make up the neck. Cervical vertebrae are the bones that make up the neck. Concavity refers to the inward curvature of a structure.
summary: The cervical spine comprises the top seven vertebrae, including the atlas and axis, which form the neck and exhibit an inward curvature.

content: Childbirth involves pelvic abduction.
summary: Pelvic abduction is a component of the childbirth process.

content: The axis is the second cervical vertebra. Cervical vertebrae are the bones that make up the neck. Cervical vertebrae are the bones that make up the neck.
summary: This chunk defines the axis (second cervical vertebra) and describes the cervical vertebrae as the bones composing the neck.

content: Concavity refers to the inward curvature of a structure.
summary: This chunk defines concavity as the inward curvature of a structure.

content: Chopart's joint is located in the foot. The cuboid bone is a bone in the foot. The cuneiform bones are bones in the foot.
summary: This chunk identifies anatomical locations of joints and bones within the foot.

content: Clavicles are bones in the shoulder girdle. The scapula is a flat bone in the shoulder girdle. The Coracoid process is a bony projection on the scapula.

content: The deltoid muscle is located in the shoulder. The pectoralis major muscle is located in the chest. The subscapularis muscle is located in the shoulder. The trapezius muscle is located in the upper back and neck. The Coracobrachialis muscle is located in the shoulder and arm. The delto-pectoral groove is a space between the deltoid and pectoralis major muscles.
summary: This chunk describes the location of various muscles in the shoulder, chest, and upper back.

content: The sternocleidomastoid muscle is located in the neck. Deep neck muscles include the sternocleidomastoid, scalenes, and longus colli.

content: Muscles affecting the hip include the gluteus maximus, medius, and minimus, as well as the hamstrings and quadriceps.
summary: This chunk lists the muscles that primarily affect hip movement.

content: The elbow joint is located in the arm. The knee joint is located in the leg.

content: Collateral ligaments are located in the knee. Cruciate ligaments are located in the knee.

content: The Coracoacromial ligament is located in the shoulder. The Coracohumeral ligament is located in the shoulder. The Coracoid ligament is located in the shoulder.

content: Costal cartilage is the flexible tissue that connects the ribs to the sternum. The costo-iliac space is the area between the ribs and the ilium.
summary: This chunk describes costal cartilage and the costo-iliac space.

content: Coxa valga is a condition in which the angle of the femoral neck is wider than normal. Coxa vara is a condition in which the angle of the femoral neck is narrower than normal.
summary: This chunk defines coxa valga and coxa vara as conditions affecting the angle of the femoral neck.

content: Cranial bones make up the skull.

content: The coronoid process is a bony projection on the ulna. The coronal plane is a vertical plane that divides the body into front and back portions.
summary: This chunk describes the coronoid process and the coronal plane.

content: Deep refers to a structure that is located far from the surface of the body. Deep back muscles include the erector spinae, quadratus lumborum, and transversus abdominis.

content: The cuneiform bones are bones in the foot. Distal phalanges are the bones of the fingertips. The diaphysis is the shaft of a long bone. Epiphysis is the rounded end of a long bone.

content: Deep refers to a structure that is located far from the surface of the body. Deep back muscles include the erector spinae, quadratus lumborum, and transversus abdominis. Deep neck muscles include the sternocleidomastoid, scalenes, and longus colli.

content: The deltoid muscle is located in the shoulder. The deltoid ligament is located in the ankle. The delto-pectoral groove is a space between the deltoid and pectoralis major muscles.
summary: This chunk describes the location and relationship of the deltoid muscle and ligament.

content: The dens is a bony projection on the axis. The elbow joint is located in the arm. The elbow joint has bones, articulating surfaces, ligaments, and muscles. The elbow joint allows for flexion and extension. The elbow joint has extensor and flexor muscles. The elbow joint has pronation and supination movements.
summary: This chunk describes the anatomy and function of the elbow joint.

content: Depression refers to a downward movement of a body part. Dorsiflexion is a movement that bends the foot upward. Downward rotation refers to a movement of the scapula. Elevation refers to an upward movement of a body part. Extension is a movement that increases the angle between two bones. Eversion is a movement that turns the sole of the foot outward.
summary: This chunk defines various types of movements in the body.

content: Diarthroses are freely movable joints. Ellipsoid joints are biaxial joints that allow for movement in two planes. Enarthroidial joints are multiaxial joints that allow for movement in multiple planes. Dislocation is a separation of two bones at a joint.
summary: This chunk describes different types of joints and a common joint injury.

content: The diaphragm is a muscle that separates the chest cavity from the abdominal cavity. The diaphragm has arcuate ligaments, a central tendon, costal origin, muscle origins, and vertebral origins. The diaphragm is involved in respiration. The diaphragm has a sternal origin.
summary: This chunk describes the anatomy and function of the diaphragm.

content: Elasticity refers to the ability of a tissue to return to its original shape after being stretched. Endomysium is the connective tissue that surrounds individual muscle fibers. Epimysium is the connective tissue that surrounds a whole muscle.
summary: This chunk defines elasticity and describes connective tissues related to muscles.

content: The extensor carpi radialis brevis muscle is located in the forearm. The extensor carpi radialis longus muscle is located in the forearm. The extensor carpi ulnaris muscle is located in the forearm. The extensor digiti minimi muscle is located in the hand. The extensor digitorum brevis muscle is located in the foot.

content: The extensor carpi radialis brevis muscle is located in the forearm. The extensor carpi radialis longus muscle is located in the forearm. The extensor carpi ulnaris muscle is located in the forearm. The extensor digiti minimi muscle is located in the hand. The extensor digitorum brevis muscle is located in the foot. The extensor digitorum longus muscle is located in the leg. The extensor hallucis longus muscle is located in the leg. The extensor indicis muscle is located in the hand. The extensor pollicis brevis muscle is located in the hand. The extensor pollicis longus muscle is located in the hand. The extensor retinaculum is a band of connective tissue that holds the extensor tendons in place.
summary: This chunk describes the location of various extensor muscles in the forearm, hand, foot, and leg.

content: Extensors are muscles that extend a joint.

summary: This chunk describes the anatomy of the thigh, including fascia lata, femoral head, femoral neck, and femur.

The lateral and medial condyles of the tibia are located on either side of the intercondylar eminence. The medial surface of the tibia is located on the inside of the tibia. Muscles affecting the hip and knee are attached to the tibia. The shaft of the tibia is the long part of the bone. The surfaces of the tibia include the anterior, medial, and posterior surfaces. The tibial plateau is a flat surface on the top of the tibia. The tibial tuberosity is a bony prominence on the tibia. The tibial tuberosity is involved in knee rotation.
summary: This chunk describes the anatomy of the tibia, including its location, surface features, and bony prominences.

content: Vertebrae are the bones that make up the spine. Vertebrae have articular facets, articular processes, spinous processes, and transverse processes. Cervical vertebrae are located in the neck. Lumbar vertebrae are located in the lower back. The movement of vertebrae is important for spinal function.
summary: Vertebrae are the bones of the spine, with different types located in the neck and lower back. Vertebral movement is crucial for spinal function.

content: The vertebral arch is a bony structure that surrounds the spinal cord. The vertebral body is the main part of the vertebra. The vertebral canal is a passageway for the spinal cord. The vertebral foramen is a hole in the vertebra that allows for the passage of the spinal cord. The vertebral structure includes the vertebral body, vertebral arch, and vertebral foramen.
summary: The vertebral structure consists of the body, arch, and foramen, which protect and support the spinal cord.

content: The vertebral column is the main support structure of the body. The vertebral artery supplies blood to the brain. Vertebral flexion should be avoided when loaded.
summary: The vertebral column is the body's primary support structure, with the vertebral artery supplying blood to the brain.  Flexion should be avoided under load.
